1 Criminal and Juvenile Justice Recodification
2026 GENERAL SESSION
STATE OF UTAH
Chief Sponsor: Todd Weiler
House Sponsor:Karianne Lisonbee
2
3 LONG TITLE
4 General Description:
5 This bill recodifies and amends provisions related to criminal and juvenile justice.
6 Highlighted Provisions:
7 This bill:
8 ▸ creates Title 75E, Criminal and Juvenile Justice Administration;
9 ▸ creates the Department of Criminal Justice within Title 75E, Criminal and Juvenile Justice
Administration;
1 1 ▸ recodifies to Title 75E, Criminal and Juvenile Justice Administration:
1 2 Title 63M, Chapter 7, Criminal Justice and Substance Abuse;
●
1 3 Title 77, Chapter 38, Part 6, Safe at Home Program;
●
1 4 Title 78B, Chapter 22, Part 4, Utah Indigent Defense Commission;

●

●
1 7 Title 78B, Chapter 22, Part 8, Child Welfare Parental Representation Program;

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 9 Title 78B, Chapter 22, Part 11, Youth Defense Fund;
●
2 0 ▸ changes the name of:
2 1 Title 76, Utah Criminal Code, to Title 76, Criminal Offenses; and
●
2 2 Title 77, Utah Code of Criminal Procedure, to Title 77, Criminal Procedure;
●
2 3 ▸ defines terms;
2 4 ▸ establishes roles and responsibilities of the Department of Criminal Justice and the commissioner
of the Department of Criminal Justice;
2 6 ▸ reassigns certain responsibilities of the Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice to the
Department of Criminal Justice;
2 8 ▸ clarifies roles, responsibilities, and administration of certain entities within the Department of
Criminal Justice;
3 0 ▸ removes obsolete programs, responsibilities, and reporting requirements of certain entities within
the Department of Criminal Justice;
3 2 ▸ revises the names of certain entities within the Department of Criminal Justice;
3 3 ▸ provides that the governor may direct the Department of Criminal Justice to assist with
extradition;

609 UNCODIFIED MATERIAL

611 Be it enacted by the Legislature of the state of Utah:
612 Section 1. Section 10-3-716 is amended to read:
613 10-3-716. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Fines and forfeitures -- Disposition.
527 (1) All fines, penalties, and forfeitures for the violation of any ordinance, when collected, shall be paid
in accordance with Section 51-4-2.
529 (2) A violation of this section constitutes a class C misdemeanor.
530
- 24 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(3) The retention or use of any fine, penalty, or forfeiture by any person for personal use or benefit
constitutes a class B misdemeanor, except that if the amount or amounts exceed $1,000 the offense
is a class A misdemeanor as defined in [the Utah Criminal Code] Title 76, Criminal Offenses.
621 Section 2. Section 13-53-111 is amended to read:
622 13-53-111. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Recidivism reporting requirements.
536 (1) On or before August 31 of each year, a residential vocational or life skills program shall collect
and report data on recidivism of participants to the [State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile
Justice] Department of Criminal Justice.
539 (2) The report described in Subsection (1) shall include the metrics and requirements described in
Section [63M-7-102] 75E-2-203.
541 (3) The [State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice] Department of Criminal Justice
shall include the information provided under this section in the report described in Subsection
[63M-7-204(1)(x)] 75E-2-202(19).
631 Section 3. Section 17-72-101 is amended to read:
632 17-72-101. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions.
As used in this chapter:
547 (1) "Commissary account" means an account from which a prisoner may withdraw money, deposited by
the prisoner or another individual, to purchase discretionary items for sale by a correctional facility.
550 (2) "Commissary purchase" means a transaction initiated by a prisoner by which the prisoner obtains an
item or items offered for sale by the correctional facility in exchange for money withdrawn from the
prisoner's commissary account.
553 [(3) "Commission" means the State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice created in Section
63M-7-201.]
555 [(4)] (3) "Correctional facility" means the same as that term is defined in Section 77-16b-102.
556 [(5)] (4) "County inmate" means an inmate who is sentenced to a county jail.
557 [(6)] (5) "Cross-sex hormone treatment" means the same as that term is defined in Section
26B-4-1001.[281-12(6)]
559 (6) "Department" means the Department of Criminal Justice created in Section 75E-2-102.
560 (7)
(a) "In-custody death" means a prisoner death that occurs while the prisoner is in the custody of a
county jail.
- 25 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
562 (b) "In-custody death" includes a prisoner death that occurs while the prisoner is:
563 (i) being transported for health care; or
564 (ii) receiving health care outside of a county jail.
565 (8) "Inmate" means a prisoner who is in the custody of a correctional facility following a criminal
conviction.
567 (9) "Medication assisted treatment plan" means a prescription plan to use prescribed medication
approved by the federal Food and Drug Administration, such as buprenorphine, methadone, or
naltrexone to treat substance use withdrawal symptoms or an opioid use disorder.
571 (10) "Notice" means all papers and orders, except process, required to be served in any proceeding
before any court, board, commission, or officer, or when required by law to be served independently
of a court proceeding.
574 (11) "Opiate" means the same as that term is defined in Section 58-37-2.
575 (12) "Primary sex characteristic surgical procedure" means the same as that term is defined in Section
26B-4-1001.
577 (13) "Prisoner" means an individual who is:
578 (a) in custody of a peace officer in accordance with a lawful arrest; or
579 (b) confined in a county jail.
580 (14) "Police interlocal entity" means the same as that term is defined in Sections 17-76-201 and
17-76-301.
582 (15) "Police special district" means the same as that term is defined in Section 17-76-201.
583 (16) "Probationer" means an individual on probation under the supervision of the county sheriff.
585 (17) "Process" means all writs, warrants, summonses and orders of the courts of justice or judicial
officers.
587 (18)
(a) "Qualifying domestic violence offense" means, except as provided in Subsection (18)(b), the same
as that term is defined in Section 77-36-1.1.
589 (b) "Qualifying domestic violence offense" does not include criminal mischief as that term is defined in
Section 76-6-106.
591 (19) "State inmate" means an inmate who is sentenced to the Department of Corrections, created in
Section 64-13-2, even if the inmate is in the custody of a county jail.
593
- 26 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(20) "Secondary sex characteristic surgical procedure" means the same as that term is defined in Section
26B-4-1001.
595 (21) "Violent felony" means the same as that term is defined in Section 76-3-203.5.
683 Section 4. Section 17-72-402 is amended to read:
684 17-72-402. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Sheriff's classification of jail facilities
-- Maximum operating capacity of jail facilities -- Limitations on contracting -- Transfer or release
of prisoners -- Limitation -- Records regarding release.
600 (1)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (5), a county sheriff shall determine:
601 (i) subject to Subsection (1)(b), the classification of each county jail facility or section of a county
jail facility under the sheriff's control;
603 (ii) the nature of each program conducted at a county jail facility under the sheriff's control; and
605 (iii) the internal operation of a county jail facility under the sheriff's control.
606 (b) A classification under Subsection (1)(a)(i) of a jail facility may not violate any applicable zoning
ordinance or conditional use permit of the county or municipality.
608 (2) Except as provided in Subsection (5), each county sheriff shall:
609 (a) with the approval of the county legislative body, establish a maximum operating capacity for each
county jail facility under the sheriff's control, based on facility design and staffing; and
612 (b) upon a county jail facility reaching the county jail facility's maximum operating capacity:
614 (i) transfer prisoners to another appropriate facility:
615 (A) under the sheriff's control; or
616 (B) available to the sheriff by contract;
617 (ii) subject to the requirements of Subsection (4), release prisoners:
618 (A) to a supervised release program, according to release criteria established by the sheriff; or
620 (B) to another alternative incarceration program developed by the sheriff; or
621 (iii) admit prisoners in accordance with law and a uniform admissions policy imposed equally upon all
entities using the county jail.
623 (3)
(a) The sheriff shall keep records of the release status and the type of release program or alternative
incarceration program for any prisoner released under Subsection (2)(b)(ii).
626
- 27 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(b) The sheriff shall make records described in Subsection (3)(a) available upon request to the
Department of Corrections, the judiciary, and the [commission] department.
628 (4) A sheriff may not release an individual due to overcrowding who, based on information that is
reasonably available to the sheriff:
630 (a) is arrested or convicted of a violent criminal offense as defined in Section 76-3-203.10;
632 (b) is arrested or convicted of a drug offense that is a felony;
633 (c) is arrested or convicted of possession of any composition or mixture, including pills, that contains
100 grams or more of fentanyl or a fentanyl-related substance;
635 (d) is arrested or convicted of an offense of driving under the influence or driving with a measurable
controlled substance in the body, if the offense results in death or serious bodily injury to an
individual;
638 (e) has been previously booked into the same jail within the 12-month period immediately before the
individual's current incarceration began; or
640 (f) has an outstanding warrant for failing to appear in a case:
641 (i) involving any charge described in Subsections (4)(a) through (4)(d); or
642 (ii) where the individual classifies as a habitual offender as defined in Section 77-18-102.
644 (5)
(a) This section may not be construed to authorize a sheriff to modify provisions of a contract with the
Department of Corrections to house in a county jail a state inmate sentenced to the Department of
Corrections.
647 (b) A county contracting with another county to house a county inmate due to capacity issues:
649 (i) shall contract with a county that:
650 (A) has available capacity in the county's county jail; and
651 (B) agrees to contract to house the county inmate;
652 (ii) shall, subject to the agreement of the parties to the contract, pay to the county contracting to receive
the transferred county inmate a day per capita rate that does not exceed the higher of:
655 (A) the current average cost of housing a county inmate in the transferring county jail; or
657 (B) the daily incarceration rates described in Section 64-13e-103.1; and
658 (iii) if the county is a county of the first class, and if the county or a sheriff in the county has released a
prisoner due to overcrowding during the lookback period described in Subsection (5)(c), the county:
661
- 28 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(A) may not enter into a new contract with a federal agency for the purpose of housing federal
detainees;
663 (B) may not house federal detainees in a number that exceeds the number of beds that the county has
contracted for with a federal agency in the current fiscal year; and
666 (C) shall publish daily totals on the public data dashboard showing:
667 (I) the total number of federal detainees held;
668 (II) the total number of beds under contract with a federal agency; and
669 (III) the total number of beds that are currently under contract with another county for the purpose of
housing federal detainees.
671 (c) The lookback period described in Subsection (5)(b)(iii) is:
672 (i) beginning on September 1, 2025, the period that begins on September 1, 2025, and ends on August
31, 2026; and
674 (ii) for September 1, 2026, forward, the period that begins on September 1 of the previous calendar year
and ends on August 31 of the current calendar year.
676 (6) Regardless of whether a county jail facility has reached the county jail facility's maximum operating
capacity under Subsection (2), a sheriff may release an individual from a county jail facility in
accordance with:
679 (a) Section 17-72-804 and Section 77-20-203; or[ ]
680 (b) Section 77-20-204.
681 (7) The sheriff of a county of the first class is encouraged to open and operate all sections of a county
jail facility within the county that are not being used to full capacity.
770 Section 5. Section 17-72-408 is amended to read:
771 17-72-408. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)County jail reporting requirements.
685 (1) Each county jail shall submit a report to the [commission] department before June 15 of each year
that includes, for the preceding calendar year:
687 (a) the average daily prisoner population each month;
688 (b) the number of prisoners in the county jail on the last day of each month who identify as each race
or ethnicity included in the Standards for Transmitting Race and Ethnicity published by the United
States Federal Bureau of Investigation;
691 (c) the number of prisoners booked into the county jail;
692
- 29 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(d) the number of prisoners held in the county jail each month on behalf of each of the following
entities:
694 (i) the Bureau of Indian Affairs;
695 (ii) a state prison;
696 (iii) a federal prison;
697 (iv) the United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement; and
698 (v) any other entity with which a county jail has entered a contract to house inmates on the entity's
behalf;
700 (e) the number of prisoners that are denied pretrial release and held in the custody of the county jail
while the prisoner awaited final disposition of the prisoner's criminal charges;
703 (f) for each prisoner booked into the county jail:
704 (i) the name of the agency that arrested the prisoner;
705 (ii) the date and time the prisoner was booked into and released from the custody of the county jail;
707 (iii) if the prisoner was released from the custody of the county jail, the reason the inmate was released
from the custody of the county jail;
709 (iv) if the prisoner was released from the custody of the county jail on a financial condition, whether the
financial condition was set by a county sheriff or a court;
711 (v) the number of days the prisoner was held in the custody of the county jail before disposition of the
prisoner's criminal charges;
713 (vi) whether the prisoner was released from the custody of the county jail before final disposition of the
prisoner's criminal charges; and
715 (vii) the prisoner's state identification number;
716 (g) the number of in-custody deaths that occurred at the county jail;
717 (h) for each in-custody death:
718 (i) the deceased's name, gender, race, ethnicity, age, and known or suspected medical diagnosis or
disability, if any;
720 (ii) the date, time, and location of death;
721 (iii) the law enforcement agency that detained, arrested, or was in the process of arresting the deceased;
and
723 (iv) a brief description of the circumstances surrounding the death;
724
- 30 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(i) the known, or discoverable on reasonable inquiry, causes and contributing factors of each of the in-
custody deaths described in Subsection (2)(g);
726 (j) the county jail's policy for notifying an inmate's next of kin after the prisoner's in-custody death;
728 (k) the county jail policies, procedures, and protocols:
729 (i) for treatment of a prisoner experiencing withdrawal from alcohol or substance use, including use of
opiates;
731 (ii) that relate to the county jail's provision, or lack of provision, of medications used to treat,
mitigate, or address a prisoner's symptoms of withdrawal, including methadone and all forms of
buprenorphine and naltrexone; and
734 (iii) that relate to screening, assessment, and treatment of a prisoner for a substance use or mental
health disorder, including the policies, procedures, and protocols that implement the requirements
described in Section 17-72-501;
737 (l)
(i) the number of prisoners whose screening described in Section 17-72-501 indicated the presence of a
substance use disorder; and
739 (ii) of the prisoners whose screening indicated the presence of a substance use disorder, the number of
prisoners who received medication under a medication assisted treatment plan; and
742 (m) any report the county jail provides or is required to provide under federal law or regulation relating
to prisoner deaths.
744 (2)
(a) Subsection (1) does not apply to a county jail if the county jail:
745 (i) collects and stores the data described in Subsection (1); and
746 (ii) enters into a memorandum of understanding with the [commission] department that allows the
[commission] department to access the data described in Subsection (1).
749 (b) The memorandum of understanding described in Subsection (2)(a)(ii) shall include a provision to
protect any information related to an ongoing investigation and comply with all applicable federal
and state laws.
752 (c) If the [commission] department accesses data from a county jail in accordance with Subsection (2)
(a), the [commission] department may not release a report prepared from that data, unless:
755 (i) the [commission] department provides the report for review to:
756 (A) the county jail; and
- 31 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
757 (B) any arresting agency that is named in the report; and
758 (ii)
(A) the county jail approves the report for release;
759 (B) the county jail reviews the report and prepares a response to the report to be published with the
report; or
761 (C) the county jail fails to provide a response to the report within four weeks after the day on which the
[commission] department provides the report to the county jail.
764 (3) The [commission] department shall:
765 (a) compile the information from the reports described in Subsection (1);
766 (b) omit or redact any identifying information of an inmate in the compilation to the extent omission or
redaction is necessary to comply with state and federal law;
768 (c) submit the compilation to the Law Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee and the
Utah Substance Use and Mental Health Advisory Committee before November 1 of each year; and
771 (d) submit the compilation to the protection and advocacy agency designated by the governor before
November 1 of each year.
773 (4) The [commission] department may not provide access to or use a county jail's policies, procedures,
or protocols submitted under this section in a manner or for a purpose not described in this section.
776 (5) Upon request, a county jail shall make a report, including only the names and causes of death
of deceased inmates and the facility in which the deceased inmates were being held in custody,
available to the public.
866 Section 6. Section 17E-2-101 is amended to read:
867 17E-2-101. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions.
As used in this chapter:
782 [(1) "Commission" means the State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice created in Section
63M-7-201.]
784 [(2)] (1) "Criminal justice agency" means an agency or institution directly involved in the apprehension,
prosecution, or incarceration of a person involved in criminal activity.
786 [(3)] (2) "Criminal justice coordinating council" or "council" means a council created by a county or
counties in accordance with Section 17E-2-201.
788
- 32 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
[(4)] (3) "Criminal justice system" means the continuum of criminal justice agencies and post-
incarceration services that an individual may encounter as a result of the individual's criminal
activity.
791 (4) "Department" means the Department of Criminal Justice created in Section 75E-2-102.
792 (5)
(a) "Post-incarceration services" means services that may assist an individual who is leaving
incarceration to reintegrate into the community.
794 (b) "Post-incarceration services" includes:
795 (i) educational services;
796 (ii) housing services;
797 (iii) health care services;
798 (iv) workforce services; and
799 (v) human services programs.
887 Section 7. Section 17E-2-201 is amended to read:
888 17E-2-201. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Criminal justice coordinating councils
-- Creation -- Strategic plan -- Reporting requirements.
803 (1)
(a) Beginning January 1, 2023, a county shall:
804 (i) create a criminal justice coordinating council; or
805 (ii) jointly with another county or counties, create a criminal justice coordinating council.
807 (b) The purpose of a council is to coordinate and improve components of the criminal justice system in
the county or counties.
809 (2)
(a) A council shall include:
810 (i) one county commissioner or county council member;
811 (ii) the county sheriff or the sheriff's designee;
812 (iii) one chief of police of a municipality within the county or the chief's designee;
813 (iv) the county attorney or the attorney's designee;
814 (v) one public defender or attorney who provides public defense within the county;
815 (vi) one district court judge;
816 (vii) one justice court judge;
- 33 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
817 (viii) one representative from the Division of Adult Probation and Parole created in Section
64-14-202;
819 (ix) one representative from the local mental health authority within the county; and
820 (x) one individual who is:
821 (A) a crime victim; or
822 (B) a victim advocate, as defined in Section 77-38-403.
823 (b) A council may include:
824 (i) an individual representing:
825 (A) local government;
826 (B) human services programs;
827 (C) higher education;
828 (D) peer support services;
829 (E) workforce services;
830 (F) local housing services;
831 (G) mental health or substance use disorder providers;
832 (H) a health care organization within the county;
833 (I) a local homeless council;
834 (J) family counseling and support groups; or
835 (K) organizations that work with families of incarcerated individuals; or
836 (ii) an individual with lived experiences in the criminal justice system.
837 (3)
(a) A member who is an elected county official shall serve as chair of the council.
838 (b) The council shall elect the member to serve as chair under Subsection (3)(a).
839 (4)
(a) A council shall develop and implement a strategic plan for the county's or counties' criminal justice
system that includes:
841 (i) mapping of all systems, resources, assets, and services within the county's or counties' criminal
justice system;
843 (ii) a plan for data sharing across the county's or counties' criminal justice system;
844 (iii) recidivism reduction objectives; and
845
- 34 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(iv) community reintegration goals, including identifying strategies for connecting county residents
who are on probation, parole, or leaving jail or prison, including those under the custody of
the Division of Juvenile Justice and Youth Services, with county-based housing, employment,
mental health services, substance use treatment, and related resources.
850 (b) The [commission] department may assist a council in the development of a strategic plan.
852 (5) As part of the council's duties described in Subsection (4)(a)(i), the council shall prepare a list of
private probation providers for a court to provide to defendants as described in Section 77-18-105.
855 (6) Before November 30 of each year, a council shall provide a written report to the
[commission] department regarding:
857 (a) the implementation of a strategic plan described in Subsection (4); and
858 (b) any data on the impact of the council on the criminal justice system in the county or counties.
947 Section 8. Section 20A-2-204 is amended to read:
948 20A-2-204. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Registering to vote when applying for
or renewing a driver license or other qualifying form.
863 (1) As used in this section, "voter registration form" means, when an individual named on a qualifying
form, as defined in Section 20A-2-108, answers "yes" to the question described in Subsection
20A-2-108(2)(a)(i), the information on the qualifying form that can be used for voter registration
purposes.
867 (2)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (2)(b), a citizen who is qualified to vote may register to vote, and
a citizen who is qualified to preregister to vote may preregister to vote, by answering "yes" to the
question described in Subsection 20A-2-108(2)(a)(i) and completing the voter registration form.
871 (b) A citizen who is a program participant in the Safe at Home Program created in Section
[77-38-602] 75E-11-102 is not eligible to register to vote as described in Subsection (2)(a), but is
eligible to register to vote by any other means described in this part.
875 (3) The Driver License Division shall:
876 (a) assist an individual in completing the voter registration form unless the individual refuses assistance;
878 (b) electronically transmit each address change to the lieutenant governor on or before the first business
day that is at least five calendar days after the day on which the division receives the address
change; and
881
- 35 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(c) on or before the first business day that is at least five calendar days after the day on which the
division receives a voter registration form, electronically transmit the form to the Office of the
Lieutenant Governor, including the following for the individual named on the form:
885 (i) the name, date of birth, driver license or state identification card number, last four digits of the social
security number, Utah residential address, place of birth, and signature;
888 (ii) a mailing address, if different from the individual's Utah residential address;
889 (iii) an email address and phone number, if available;
890 (iv) the desired political affiliation, if indicated;
891 (v) an indication of whether the individual requested that the individual's voter registration record be
classified as a private record under Subsection 20A-2-108(2)(b); and
894 (vi) a withholding request form described in Subsections 20A-2-104(7) and (8) and any verification
submitted with the form.
896 (4) Upon receipt of an individual's voter registration form from the Driver License Division under
Subsection (3), the lieutenant governor shall:
898 (a) enter the information into the statewide voter registration database; and
899 (b) if the individual requests on the individual's voter registration form that the individual's voter
registration record be classified as a private record or the individual submits a withholding request
form described in Subsections 20A-2-104(7) and (8) and any required verification, classify the
individual's voter registration record as a private record.
904 (5) The county clerk of an individual whose information is entered into the statewide voter registration
database under Subsection (4) shall:
906 (a) ensure that the individual meets the qualifications to be registered or preregistered to vote; and
908 (b)
(i) if the individual meets the qualifications to be registered to vote:
909 (A) ensure that the individual is assigned to the proper voting precinct; and
910 (B) send the individual the notice described in Section 20A-2-304; or
911 (ii) if the individual meets the qualifications to be preregistered to vote, process the form in accordance
with the requirements of Section 20A-2-101.1.
913 (6)
(a) When the county clerk receives a correctly completed voter registration form under this section, the
clerk shall:
- 36 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
915 (i) comply with the applicable provisions of this Subsection (6); or
916 (ii) if the individual is preregistering to vote, comply with Section 20A-2-101.1.
917 (b) If the county clerk receives a correctly completed voter registration form under this section no later
than 5 p.m. or, if submitting the form electronically, midnight, 11 calendar days before the date of
an election, the county clerk shall:
920 (i) accept the voter registration form; and
921 (ii) unless the individual is preregistering to vote:
922 (A) enter the individual's name on the list of registered voters for the voting precinct in which the
individual resides; and
924 (B) notify the individual that the individual is registered to vote in the upcoming election; and
926 (iii) if the individual named in the form is preregistering to vote, comply with Section 20A-2-101.1.
928 (c) If the county clerk receives a correctly completed voter registration form under this section after the
deadline described in Subsection (6)(b), the county clerk shall, unless the individual named in the
form is preregistering to vote:
931 (i) accept the application for registration of the individual;
932 (ii) process the voter registration form; and
933 (iii) unless the individual is preregistering to vote, and except as provided in Subsection 20A-2-207(6),
inform the individual that the individual will not be registered to vote in the pending election, unless
the individual registers to vote by provisional ballot during the early voting period, if applicable, or
on election day, in accordance with Section 20A-2-207.
938 (7)
(a) If the county clerk determines that an individual's voter registration form received from the Driver
License Division is incorrect because of an error, because the form is incomplete, or because the
individual does not meet the qualifications to be registered to vote, the county clerk shall mail
notice to the individual stating that the individual has not been registered or preregistered because
of an error, because the registration form is incomplete, or because the individual does not meet the
qualifications to be registered to vote.
945 (b) If a county clerk believes, based upon a review of a voter registration form, that an individual,
who knows that the individual is not legally entitled to register or preregister to vote, may be
intentionally seeking to register or preregister to vote, the county clerk shall refer the form to the
county attorney for investigation and possible prosecution.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1051 (a) require the installation or maintenance of a carbon monoxide detector in a residential dwelling
against anyone other than the occupant of the dwelling; or
1053 (b) control the production, processing, distribution, or sale price of local food in response to a public

1055 (7)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (7)(c), a local health department may not declare a public health
emergency until the local health department has provided notice of the proposed action to the chief
executive officer of the relevant county no later than 24 hours before the local health department
issues the order or declaration.
1059 (b) The local health department:
1060 (i) shall provide the notice required by Subsection (7)(a) using the best available method under the
circumstances as determined by the local health department;
1062 (ii) may provide the notice required by Subsection (7)(a) in electronic format; and
1063 (iii) shall provide the notice in written form, if practicable.
1064 (c)
(i) Notwithstanding Subsection (7)(a), a local health department may declare a public health emergency
without approval of the chief executive officer of the relevant county if the passage of time
necessary to obtain approval of the chief executive officer of the relevant county as required in
Subsection (7)(a) would substantially increase the likelihood of loss of life due to an imminent
threat.
1069 (ii) If a local health department declares a public health emergency as described in Subsection (7)(c)
(i), the local health department shall notify the chief executive officer of the relevant county before
declaring a public health emergency.
1072 (iii) The chief executive officer of the relevant county may terminate a declaration of a public health
emergency as described in Subsection (7)(c)(i) within 72 hours of declaration of the public health
emergency.
1075 (d)
(i) The relevant county governing body may at any time terminate a public health emergency issued by
the local health department by majority vote of the county governing body.
1078 (ii) A vote by the relevant county governing body to terminate a public health emergency as described
in Subsection (7)(d)(i) is not subject to veto by the relevant chief executive officer.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1081 (8)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (8)(b), a public health emergency declared by a local health
department expires at the earliest of:
1083 (i) the local health department or the chief executive officer of the relevant county finding that the
threat or danger has passed or the public health emergency reduced to the extent that emergency
conditions no longer exist;
1086 (ii) 30 days after the date on which the local health department declared the public health
emergency; or
1088 (iii) the day on which the public health emergency is terminated by majority vote of the county
governing body.
1090 (b)
(i) The relevant county legislative body, by majority vote, may extend a public health emergency for a
time period designated by the county legislative body.
1092 (ii) If the county legislative body extends a public health emergency as described in Subsection (8)(b)
(i), the public health emergency expires on the date designated by the county legislative body.
1095 (c) Except as provided in Subsection (8)(d), if a public health emergency declared by a local health
department expires as described in Subsection (8)(a), the local health department may not declare
a public health emergency for the same illness or occurrence that precipitated the previous public
health emergency declaration.
1099 (d)
(i) Notwithstanding Subsection (8)(c), subject to Subsection (8)(f), if the local health department
finds that exigent circumstances exist, after providing notice to the county legislative body, the
department may declare a new public health emergency for the same illness or occurrence that
precipitated a previous public health emergency declaration.
1104 (ii) A public health emergency declared as described in Subsection (8)(d)(i) expires in accordance with
Subsection (8)(a) or (b).

Title 26B, Chapter 7, Part 3, Treatment, Isolation, and Quarantine Procedures for Communicable
Diseases, the Legislature may terminate by joint resolution a public health emergency that was
declared based on exigent circumstances or that has been in effect for more than 30 days.

1313 (1)

1892 (A) present a public safety concern to the operator or any of the occupants in the vehicle; or
1894 (B) prevent the division or the peace officer from addressing other public safety considerations.
1896 (b) The division or any peace officer may not seize and take possession of a vehicle under Subsection
(4)(a):
1898 (i) if the operator of the vehicle is not carrying evidence of owner's or operator's security as defined
in Section 41-12a-303.2 in the vehicle unless the division or peace officer verifies that owner's or
operator's security is not in effect for the vehicle through the Uninsured Motorist Identification
Database created in accordance with Section 41-12a-803; or
1903 (ii) if the operator of the vehicle is carrying evidence of owner's or operator's security as defined in
Section 41-12a-303.2 in the vehicle and the Uninsured Motorist Identification Database created in
accordance with Section 41-12a-803 indicates that the owner's or operator's security is not in effect
for the vehicle, unless the division or a peace officer makes a reasonable attempt to independently
verify that owner's or operator's security is not in effect for the vehicle.
1909 (5) If necessary for the transportation of a seized vessel, the vessel's trailer may be seized to transport
and store the vessel.
1911 (6) Any peace officer seizing or taking possession of a vehicle, vessel, or outboard motor under this
section shall comply with the provisions of Section 41-6a-1406.
1913 (7)
- 66 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) In accordance with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act, the commission
shall make rules setting standards for public garages, impound lots, and impound yards that may be
used by peace officers and the division.
1916 (b) The standards shall be equitable, reasonable, and unrestrictive as to the number of public garages,
impound lots, or impound yards per geographical area.
1918 (c) A crusher, dismantler, or salvage dealer may not operate as a state impound yard unless the crusher,
dismantler, or salvage dealer meets all of the requirements for a state impound yard set forth in this
section and rules made in accordance with Subsection (7)(a).
1922 (d)
(i) Rules made by the commission shall include a requirement that a state impound yard have opaque
fencing on any side of the state impound yard that has frontage with a highway.
1925 (ii) The opaque fencing described in Subsection (7)(d)(i) may be opaque chain link fencing.
1927 (8)
(a) Except as provided under Subsection (8)(b), a person may not operate or allow to be operated a
vehicle stored in a public garage, impound lot, or impound yard regulated under this part without
prior written permission of the owner of the vehicle.
1930 (b) Incidental and necessary operation of a vehicle to move the vehicle from one parking space to
another within the facility and that is necessary for the normal management of the facility is not
prohibited under Subsection (8)(a).
1933 (9) A person who violates the provisions of Subsection (8) is guilty of a class C misdemeanor.
1935 (10) The division or the peace officer who seizes a vehicle shall record the mileage shown on the
vehicle's odometer at the time of seizure, if:
1937 (a) the vehicle is equipped with an odometer; and
1938 (b) the odometer reading is accessible to the division or the peace officer.

2388 Section 24. Section 51-9-412 is amended to read:
2389 51-9-412. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Halfway house funding -- Uses.
2303 (1) As used in this section:
2304 [(a) "Commission" means the Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice created in Section
63M-7-201.]
2306 (a) "Department" means the Department of Criminal Justice created in Section 75E-2-102.
2308 (b) "Halfway house" means a facility that houses parolees upon release from prison or houses
probationers who have violated the terms of their probation.
2310 (c) "Law enforcement agency" means a local law enforcement agency.
2311 (d) "Parole violator center" means a facility that houses parolees who have violated the conditions of
their parole agreement.
2313
- 78 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(2) The [commission] department shall allocate funds appropriated by the Legislature to local law
enforcement agencies on a pro-rata basis determined by:
2315 (a) the average daily number of occupied beds in a halfway house in each agency's jurisdiction for
increased enforcement in areas with halfway houses;
2317 (b) the average daily number of occupied beds in a parole violator center in each agency's jurisdiction;
or
2319 (c) both Subsections (2)(a) and (b).
2320 (3) A law enforcement agency may use funds received under this section only for the purposes stated in
this section.
2322 (4)
(a) For each fiscal year, any law enforcement agency that receives funds from the
[commission] department under this section shall prepare, and file with the
[commission] department and the state auditor, a report in a form specified by the
[commission] department.[ ]
2326 (b) The report described in Subsection (4)(a) shall include the following:
2327 [(a)] (i) the agency's name;
2328 [(b)] (ii) the amount received;
2329 [(c)] (iii) how the funds were used, including the impact on crime reduction efforts in areas with
halfway houses or parole violator centers, or both; and
2331 [(d)] (iv) a statement signed by both the agency's or political subdivision's executive officer or designee
and by the agency's legal counsel that all funds were used for law enforcement operations related to
reducing criminal activity in areas with halfway houses or parole violator centers, or both.

2805 (ii) commitment to a secure correctional facility operated by or under contract with the Department of
Corrections;
2807 (iii) release from confinement to parole status, termination or expiration of sentence, or escape;
2809 (iv) entrance to and release from any community-based residential program operated by or under
contract with the Department of Corrections; or
2811 (v) termination of probation or parole; and
2812 (b)
(i) for an offender convicted after May 7, 2025, of an offense committed in this state that requires the
individual to register as a sex offender, conduct, if available, multi-domain assessments that are
validated for the population and offense type of the offender to inform the treatment and supervision
needs of the offender; and
2816 (ii) 30 days after the day on which a calendar quarterly period ends, submit the results of any risk
assessments completed under Subsection (3)(b)(i) during the preceding quarter to the [State
Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice] Department of Criminal Justice.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01

of Corrections and is confined in a correctional facility not operated by or under contract with the
Department of Corrections upon:
2824 (a) commitment to the correctional facility; and

3305 Section 45. Section 58-11a-503 is amended to read:

3220
- 105 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(1) Unless Subsection (2) applies, an individual who commits an act of unlawful conduct under Section

is guilty of a class A misdemeanor.
3223 (2) Sexual conduct that violates Section 58-11a-502 and [Title 76, Utah Criminal Code] Title
76, Criminal Offenses, shall be subject to the applicable penalties in [Title 76, Utah Criminal
Code] Title 76, Criminal Offenses.
3226 (3) Grounds for immediate suspension of an individual's license or permit by the division include the
issuance of a citation for violation of Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6).
3229 (4) If upon inspection or investigation, the division concludes that an individual has violated the
provisions of Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6), or a rule or order issued with respect to
Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6), and that disciplinary action is appropriate, the director
or the director's designee from within the division shall promptly issue a citation to the individual
according to this chapter and any pertinent rules, attempt to negotiate a stipulated settlement, or
notify the individual to appear before an adjudicative proceeding conducted under Title 63G,
Chapter 4, Administrative Procedures Act.
3237 (5) An individual that is in violation of Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6), as evidenced by
an uncontested citation, a stipulated settlement, or finding of violation in an adjudicative proceeding,
may be assessed a fine in accordance with this Subsection (5) and may, in addition to or in lieu of a
fine, be ordered to cease and desist from violating Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6).
3242 (6) Except for a cease and desist order, the licensure sanctions described in Section 58-11a-401 may not
be assessed through a citation.
3244 (7)
(a) Each citation shall be in writing and describe with particularity the nature of the violation, including
a reference to the provision of the chapter, rule, or order alleged to have been violated.
3247 (b) The citation shall clearly state that the recipient [must] shall notify the division in writing within
20 calendar days of service of the citation if the recipient wishes to contest the citation at a hearing
conducted under Title 63G, Chapter 4, Administrative Procedures Act.
3251 (c) The citation shall clearly explain the consequences of failure to timely contest the citation or to
make payment of a fine assessed by the citation within the time specified in the citation.
3254 (d) Each citation issued under this section, or a copy of each citation, may be served upon an individual
upon whom a summons may be served in accordance with the Utah Rules of Civil Procedure and
- 106 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
may be made personally or upon the individual's agent by a division investigator or by an individual
specially designated by the director or by mail.
3259 (e)
(i) If within 20 calendar days from the service of a citation, the individual to which the citation was
issued fails to request a hearing to contest the citation, the citation becomes the final order of the
division and is not subject to further agency review.
3262 (ii) The period to contest a citation may be extended by the division for cause.
3263 (f) The division may refuse to issue or renew, suspend, revoke, or place on probation the license or
permit of an individual that fails to comply with a citation after the citation becomes final.
3266 (g) The failure of an applicant for licensure to comply with a citation after the citation becomes final is
a ground for denial of license.
3268 (h) The director or the director's designee from within the division may not issue a citation under this
section more than one year after the date on which the violation that is the subject of the citation is
reported to the division.
3271 (i) The director or the director's designee shall assess fines as follows:
3272 (i) for a first offense under Subsection (4), a fine of up to $1,000;
3273 (ii) for a second offense under Subsection (4), a fine of up to $2,000; and
3274 (iii) for any subsequent offense under Subsection (4), a fine of up to $2,000 for each day of continued
offense.
3276 (j) For purposes of issuing a final order under this section and assessing a fine under Subsection (7)(i),
an offense constitutes a second or subsequent offense if:
3278 (i) the division previously issued a final order determining that an individual committed a first or
second offense in violation of Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6); or
3281 (ii)
(A) the division initiated an action for a first or second offense;
3282 (B) no final order has been issued by the division in the action initiated under Subsection (7)(j)(ii)(A);
3284 (C) the division determines during an investigation that occurred after the initiation of the action
under Subsection (7)(j)(ii)(A) that the individual committed a second or subsequent violation of
Subsection 58-11a-502(1), (3), (4), (5), or (6); and
3288 (D) after determining that the individual committed a second or subsequent offense under Subsection
(7)(j)(ii)(C), the division issues a final order on the action initiated under Subsection (7)(j)(ii)(A).
- 107 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
3291 (k) In issuing a final order for a second or subsequent offense under Subsection (7)(j), the division shall
comply with the requirements of this section.
3293 (8)
(a) A penalty imposed by the director under Subsection (7)(i) shall be deposited into the Cosmetology
and Associated Professions Education and Enforcement Fund.
3295 (b) The director may collect an unpaid penalty by:
3296 (i) referring the matter to a collection agency; or
3297 (ii) bringing an action in the district court of the county in which the individual against whom the
penalty is imposed resides or in the county where the office of the director is located.
3300 (c) A county attorney or the attorney general of the state shall provide legal assistance and advice to the
director in an action to collect a penalty.
3302 (d) A court shall award reasonable attorney fees and costs to the prevailing party in an action brought
by the division to collect a penalty.

3722 (A) every well or wells; and
3723 (B) every field in which the well or wells are located;
3724 (ii) the number of barrels of oil, the cubic feet of gas, and quantity of other hydrocarbon substances
produced, including the percentage of production from lands held in trust by the United States for
any federally recognized Indian tribe or [its ] tribe members;
3728 (iii) the value of the oil or gas; and
3729 (iv) any other reasonable and necessary information required by the commission.
3730 (2) The statements or reports required to be filed with the commission shall be signed and sworn to by
the producer or a designee.

(3) Any willful false swearing as to the purported material facts set out in this report constitutes the

accrued [them] the hours; and

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(d) "Elected official" means any person who is an employee of the state because the person was elected
by the registered voters of Utah to a position in state government.
3827 (e) "Exempt employee" means a state employee who is exempt as defined by the FLSA.
3828 (f) "FLSA" means the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1978, 29 U.S.C. Sec. 201 et seq.
3829 (g) "FLSA agreement" means the agreement authorized by the FLSA by which a nonexempt employee
elects the form of compensation the nonexempt employee will receive for overtime.
3832 (h) "Nonexempt employee" means a state employee who is nonexempt as defined by the division
applying FLSA requirements.
3834 (i) "Overtime" means actual time worked in excess of an employee's defined work period.
3836 (j) "Overtime year" means the year determined by a department under Subsection (5)(b) at the end of
which an exempt employee's accrued overtime lapses.
3838 (k) "State employee" means every person employed by a department who is not:
3839 (i) an appointed official;
3840 (ii) an elected official; or
3841 (iii) a member of a board or commission who is paid only for per diem or travel expenses.

3845 (m) "Work period" means:
3846 (i) for a nonexempt employee, except a nonexempt law enforcement or hospital employee, a
consecutive seven day, 24 hour work period of 40 hours;
3848 (ii) for an exempt employee, a 14 day, 80 hour payroll cycle;
3849 (iii) for a nonexempt hospital employee, the period the division establishes by rule according to the
requirements of the FLSA; or
3851 (iv) for a nonexempt law enforcement employee as defined in the FLSA:
3852 (A) who is employed by the Department of Natural Resources, the period the division establishes by
rule according to the requirements of the FLSA; or
3854 (B) who is employed by a department other than the Department of Natural Resources, the period the
division establishes by rule in accordance with Subsection (2).
3857 (2) Except for the Department of Natural Resources, the division shall require each department
employing a nonexempt law enforcement employee to designate one of the following work periods
applicable to that employee:
3860 (a) 80 hours in a 14 consecutive day payroll cycle; or
- 124 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
3861 (b) 160 hours in a 28 consecutive day payroll cycle.
3862 (3) Each department shall compensate each state employee who works overtime by complying with the
requirements of this section.
3864 (4)
(a) Each department shall negotiate and obtain a signed FLSA agreement from each nonexempt
employee.
3866 (b) In the FLSA agreement, the nonexempt employee shall elect either to be compensated for overtime
by:
3868 (i) taking time off work at the rate of one and one-half hour off for each overtime hour worked; or
3870 (ii) being paid for the overtime worked at the rate of one and one-half times the employee's regular
hourly wage.
3872 (c) A nonexempt employee who elects to take time off under this Subsection (4) shall be paid for any
overtime worked in excess of the cap established by the division.
3874 (d) Before working any overtime, a nonexempt employee shall obtain authorization to work overtime
from the employee's immediate supervisor.
3876 (e) Each department shall:
3877 (i) for an employee who elects to be compensated with time off for overtime, allow overtime earned
during a fiscal year to be accumulated; and
3879 (ii) for an employee who elects to be paid for overtime worked, pay them for overtime worked in the
paycheck for the pay period in which the employee worked the overtime.
3882 (f) If a department pays a nonexempt employee for overtime, that department shall charge that payment
to that department's budget.
3884 (g) At the end of each fiscal year, the Division of Finance shall total all the accrued overtime hours for
nonexempt employees and charge that total against the appropriate fund or subfund.
3887 (5)
(a)
(i) Except as provided in Subsection (5)(a)(ii), each department shall compensate each exempt
employee who works overtime by granting the employee time off at the rate of one hour off for
each hour of overtime worked.
3890 (ii) The director of the division may grant limited exceptions to the compensation requirement
described in Subsection (5)(a)(i), where work circumstances dictate, by authorizing a
- 125 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
department to pay an exempt employee for overtime worked at the employee's regular hourly
wage if that department has funds available.
3894 (b)
(i) Each department shall:
3895 (A) establish in [its] the department's written human resource policies a uniform annual date for
each division that is at the end of any pay period; and
3897 (B) communicate the uniform annual date to [its] the department's employees.
3898 (ii) If any department fails to establish a uniform annual date as required by this Subsection (5), the
director of the division, in conjunction with the director of the Division of Finance, shall establish
the date for that department.
3901 (c) The overtime authorized for an exempt employee under this Subsection (5) is not an entitlement, a
benefit, or a vested right.
3903 (d) At the end of the overtime year, upon transfer to another department at any time, and upon
termination, retirement, or other situations where the employee will not return to work before the
end of the overtime year:
3906 (i) any of an exempt employee's overtime that is more than the maximum established by division rule
lapses; and
3908 (ii) unless authorized by the director of the division under Subsection (5)(a)(ii), a department may not
compensate the exempt employee for that lapsed overtime by paying the employee for the overtime
or by granting the employee time off for the lapsed overtime.
3912 (e) Before working any overtime, each exempt employee shall obtain authorization to work overtime
from the exempt employee's immediate supervisor.
3914 (f) If a department pays an exempt employee for overtime under authorization from the director of the
division, that department shall charge that payment to that department's budget in the pay period
earned.

3918 (a) ensure that the provisions of the FLSA and this section are implemented throughout state
government;
3920 (b) determine, for each state employee, whether the employee is exempt, nonexempt, law enforcement,
or has some other status under the FLSA;

4045 Section 54. Section 63G-2-305 is amended to read:
4046 63G-2-305. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Protected records.
The following records are protected if properly classified by a governmental entity:

3968 (b) the person submitting the information has a greater interest in prohibiting access than the public in
obtaining access; and

specified in Section 63G-2-309;

Legislature except as necessary to inventory or conserve items of historical significance owned by
the state.

or in the custody of a state agency, official, or employee having an office in a building on capitol
hill.
4893 (d) Except for items identified by the board as having historical significance, and except as provided
in Subsection (6)(b), the board and the employees of the board have no control over moveable
furnishings and equipment in the custody of a state agency, official, or employee having an office in
a building on capitol hill.
4985 Section 62. Section 64-13-6 is amended to read:
4986 64-13-6. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Department duties.
4899 (1) The department shall:
- 155 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
4900 (a) protect the public through institutional care and confinement, and supervision in the community of
offenders where appropriate;
4902 (b) implement court-ordered punishment of offenders;
4903 (c) provide evidence-based and evidence-informed program opportunities for offenders designed to
reduce offenders' criminogenic and recidivism risks, including behavioral, cognitive, educational,
and career-readiness program opportunities;
4906 (d) ensure that offender participation in all program opportunities described in Subsection (1)(c) is
voluntary;
4908 (e) where appropriate, utilize offender volunteers as mentors in the program opportunities described in
Subsection (1)(c);
4910 (f) provide treatment for sex offenders who are found to be treatable based upon criteria developed by
the department;
4912 (g) provide the results of ongoing clinical assessment of sex offenders and objective diagnostic testing
to sentencing and release authorities;
4914 (h) manage programs that take into account the needs and interests of victims, where reasonable;
4916 (i) through the Division of Adult Probation and Parole created in Section 64-14-202, supervise

Pardons and Parole;
4919 (j) subject to Subsection (2), investigate criminal conduct involving offenders incarcerated in a state
correctional facility;
4921 (k) cooperate and exchange information with other state, local, and federal law enforcement agencies to
achieve greater success in prevention and detection of crime and apprehension of criminals;
4924 (l) implement the provisions of Title 77, Chapter 28c, Interstate Compact for Adult Offender
Supervision;
4926 (m) establish a case action plan based on appropriate validated risk, needs, and responsivity assessments
for each offender as follows:
4928 (i)
(A) if an offender is to be supervised in the community, the department shall establish a case action
plan for the offender no later than 60 days after the day on which the department's community
supervision of the offender begins; and
4931
- 156 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(B) if the offender is committed to the custody of the department, the department shall establish a case
action plan for the offender no later than 90 days after the day on which the offender is committed to
the custody of the department;
4934 (ii) each case action plan shall:
4935 (A) integrate an individualized, evidence-based, and evidence-informed treatment and program plan
with clearly defined completion requirements; and
4937 (B) require that a case manager will:
4938 (I) ensure that an assessment of the education level, occupational interests, and aptitudes of the inmate
has been completed;
4940 (II) refer the inmate to a higher education student advisor at an institution offering programs consistent
with the inmate's interests and aptitudes for advisement on educational preferences and plans;
4943 (III) incorporate the inmate's interests, aptitudes, and student advisement into an education plan
consistent with the guidance provided by the Higher Education and Corrections Council created in
Section 53H-1-604; and
4946 (IV) refer the inmate to the student advisor at the institution called for in the case action plan for
guidance and assistance with the education process;
4948 (iii) the department shall share each newly established case action plan with the sentencing and release
authority within 30 days after the day on which the case action plan is established; and
4951 (iv) the department shall share any changes to a case action plan, including any change in an offender's
risk assessment, with the sentencing and release authority within 30 days after the day of the
change;
4954 (n) ensure that an inmate has reasonable access to legal research;

4959 (i) under this title;
4960 (ii) by the department; or

[63M-7-102] 75E-2-203;

4998 (i) "Accounts receivable" means any amount owed by an offender arising from a criminal judgment
that has not been paid.
5000 (ii) "Accounts receivable" includes unpaid fees, overpayments, fines, forfeitures, surcharges, costs,
interest, penalties, restitution to victims, third-party claims, claims, reimbursement of a reward,
and damages that an offender is ordered to pay.
5004 (b) The department shall collect and disburse, with any interest and any other costs assessed under

5006 (i) the parole period and any extension of that period in accordance with Subsection (4)(c); and

5011 (c)
(i) If an offender has an unpaid balance of the offender's accounts receivable at the time that the
offender's sentence expires or terminates, the department shall be referred to the sentencing court
for the sentencing court to enter a civil judgment of restitution and a civil accounts receivable as
described in Section 77-18-114.
5015 (ii) If the board makes an order for restitution within 60 days from the day on which the offender's
sentence expires or terminates, the board shall refer the order for restitution to the sentencing court
to be entered as a civil judgment of restitution as described in Section 77-18-114.
5019 (d) This Subsection (4) only applies to offenders sentenced before July 1, 2021.

reduce duplication of services;
5027 (ii) notify authorized users of incoming service requests or referrals;

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01

5032 (v) send and track individual referrals, store referral outcomes, and document services provided.
5122 Section 63. Section 64-13-14.5 is amended to read:
5123 64-13-14.5. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Limits of confinement place -- Release
status -- Work release.
5037 (1) The department may extend the limits of the place of confinement of an inmate when, as established
by department policies and procedures, there is cause to believe the inmate will honor the trust, by
authorizing the inmate under prescribed conditions:
5040 (a) to leave temporarily for purposes specified by department policies and procedures to visit
specifically designated places for a period not to exceed 30 days;
5042 (b) to participate in a voluntary training program in the community while housed at a correctional
facility or to work at paid employment;
5044 (c) to be housed in a nonsecure community correctional center operated by the department; or
5046 (d) to be housed in any other facility under contract with the department.
5047 (2)
(a) The department shall establish rules governing offenders on release status.
5048 (b) A copy of the rules established under Subsection (2)(a) shall be furnished to the offender and to any
employer or other person participating in the offender's release program.
5051 (c) Any employer or other participating person shall agree in writing to abide by the rules established
under Subsection (2)(a) and to notify the department of the offender's discharge or other release
from a release program activity, or of any violation of the rules governing release status.
5055 (3) The willful failure of an inmate to remain within the extended limits of his confinement or to return
within the time prescribed to an institution or facility designated by the department is an escape
from custody.
5058 (4) If an offender is arrested for the commission of a crime, the arresting authority shall immediately
notify the department of the arrest.
5060 (5) The department may impose appropriate sanctions [pursuant to] in accordance with Section
64-14-204 upon offenders who violate the adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines,
- 160 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
as defined in Section [63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101, including prosecution for escape under Section
76-8-309 or 76-8-309.3 and for absconding from supervision.
5064 (6) An inmate who is housed at a nonsecure correctional facility and on work release may not be
required to work for less than the current federally established minimum wage, or under substandard
working conditions.
5155 Section 64. Section 64-13-14.7 is amended to read:
5156 64-13-14.7. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Victim notification of offender's
release.
5069 (1) As used in this section:
5070 (a) "Offender" means [a person] an individual who committed an act of criminally injurious conduct
against the victim and has been sentenced to incarceration in the custody of the department.
5073 (b)
(i) "Victim" means [a person] an individual against whom an offender committed criminally injurious
conduct as defined in Section [63M-7-502] 75E-5-101, and who is entitled to notice of hearings
regarding the offender's parole under Section 77-27-9.5.[ ]
5077 (ii) "Victim" includes the legal guardian of a victim, or the representative of the family of a victim who
is deceased.
5079 (2)
(a)
(i) Upon submitting a signed written request of notification to the Department of Corrections,
a victim shall be notified of an offender's release under [Sections ] Section 64-13-14.5 and
[64-13-14.7] this section, or any other release to or from a half-way house, to a program outside
of the prison such as a rehabilitation program, state hospital, community center other than a
release on parole, commutation, or termination for which notice is provided under Sections
77-27-9.5 and 77-27-9.7, transfer of the offender to an out-of-state facility, an offender's escape,
or an offender's termination from probation or parole.
5087 (ii) The request shall include a current mailing address and may include current telephone numbers
if the victim chooses.
5089 (iii) The notice for an offender's termination from probation or parole shall notify the victim that the
victim may petition the court for the appropriate continuous protective order under Subsection
78B-7-804(5) or 78B-7-805(5).

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
5092 (b)
(i) Subject to Subsection (2)(b)(ii), the department shall advise the victim of an offender's release or
escape under Subsection (2)(a), in writing.
5094 (ii) If written notice is not feasible because the release is immediate or the offender escapes, the
department shall make a reasonable attempt to notify the victim by telephone if the victim has
provided a telephone number under Subsection (2)(a) and shall follow up with a written notice.
5098 (3)
(a) Notice of victim rights under this section shall be provided to the victim in the notice of hearings
regarding parole under Section 77-27-9.5.
5100 (b) The department shall coordinate with the Board of Pardons and Parole to ensure the notice is
implemented.

section is private information that the department may not release:
5104 (a) to the offender under any circumstances; or
5105 (b) to any other party without the written consent of the victim.
5106 (5) The department may make rules as necessary to implement this section.
5107 (6) The department or [its] the department's employees acting within the scope of their employment
are not civilly or criminally liable for failure to provide notice or improper notice under this section
unless the failure or impropriety is willful or grossly negligent.

5280 Section 66. Section 64-13-25 is amended to read:
5281 64-13-25. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Standards for programs -- Audits.
5194 (1)
(a) To promote accountability and to ensure safe and professional operation of correctional programs,
the department shall establish minimum standards for the organization and operation of the
department's programs, including collaborating with the Department of Health and Human Services
to establish minimum standards for programs providing assistance for individuals involved in the
criminal justice system.
5200 (b)
(i) The department shall [promulgate] establish the standards according to state rulemaking provisions.
5202 (ii) Those standards that apply to offenders are exempt from the provisions of Title 63G, Chapter 3,
Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act.
5204 (iii) Offenders are not a class of persons under Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking
Act.
5206 (c) The standards shall provide for inquiring into and processing offender complaints.
5207 (d)
(i) The department shall establish minimum standards and qualifications for treatment programs
provided in county jails to which persons committed to the state prison are placed by jail contract
under Section 64-13e-103.
5210 (ii) In establishing the standards and qualifications for the treatment programs, the department shall:
5212 (A) consult and collaborate with the county sheriffs and the Office of Substance Use and Mental
Health; and
5214 (B) include programs demonstrated by recognized scientific research to reduce recidivism by
addressing an offender's criminal risk factors as determined by a risk and needs assessment.
- 165 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
5217 (iii) All jails contracting to house offenders committed to the state prison shall meet the minimum
standards for treatment programs as established under this Subsection (1)(d).
5220 (e)
(i) The department shall establish minimum standards for sex offense treatment, which shall include the
requirements under Subsection 64-13-7.5(3) regarding licensure and competency.
5223 (ii) The standards shall require the use of evidence-based practices to address criminal risk factors as
determined by validated assessments.
5225 (iii) The department shall collaborate with the Office of Substance Use and Mental Health to develop
and effectively distribute the standards to jails and to mental health professionals who desire to
provide mental health treatment for sex offenders.
5229 (iv) The department shall establish the standards by administrative rule in accordance with Title 63G,
Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act.
5231 (2)
(a) The department shall establish a certification process for public and private providers of treatment
for sex offenders on probation or parole that requires the providers' sex offense treatment practices
meet the standards and practices established under Subsection (1)(e)(i) with the goal of reducing sex
offender recidivism.
5236 (b) The department shall collaborate with the Office of Substance Use and Mental Health to develop,
coordinate, and implement the certification process.
5238 (c) The department shall base the certification process on the standards under Subsection (1)(e)(i) and
require renewal of certification every two years.
5240 (d) All public and private providers of sex offense treatment, including those providing treatment
to offenders housed in county jails by contract under Section 64-13e-103, shall comply with the
standards in order to begin receiving or continue receiving payment from the department to provide
sex offense treatment.
5244 (e) The department shall establish the certification program by administrative rule in accordance with
Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act.
5246 (3) The department:
5247 (a) shall establish performance goals and outcome measurements for all programs that are subject to the
minimum standards established under this section and collect data to analyze and evaluate whether
the goals and measurements are attained;
- 166 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
5250 (b) shall collaborate with the Office of Substance Use and Mental Health to develop and coordinate the
performance goals and outcome measurements, including recidivism rates and treatment success and
failure rates;
5253 (c) may use the data collected under Subsection (3)(b) to make decisions on the use of funds to provide
treatment for which standards are established under this section;
5255 (d) shall collaborate with the Office of Substance Use and Mental Health to track a subgroup of
participants to determine if there is a net positive result from the use of treatment as an alternative to
incarceration;
5258 (e) shall collaborate with the Office of Substance Use and Mental Health to evaluate the costs, including
any additional costs, and the resources needed to attain the performance goals established for the use

5262 (f) shall annually provide data collected under this Subsection (3) to the [State Commission on Criminal

5515 Section 69. Section 64-13e-103.1 is amended to read:
5516 64-13e-103.1. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Calculating the average state daily
incarceration cost and the state incarceration rate.
5430 (1) Before September 15 of each year, the department shall:
5431 (a) calculate the average state daily incarceration cost;[ and:]
5432 [(i)] (b)
(i) if the average state daily incarceration cost calculated in Subsection (1)(a) equals more than 105% of
the previous year's state daily incarceration rate:
5434 (A) set the state daily incarceration rate at 105% of the prior year's state daily incarceration rate;
and
5436 (B) record that year's rate surplus; or
5437 (ii) if the average state daily incarceration cost calculated in Subsection (1)(a) is less than 105% of the
previous year's state daily incarceration rate:
5439 (A) set the state daily incarceration rate at the state daily incarceration cost; or
5440 (B) if in any one or more of the prior three years there existed a rate surplus, and that rate surplus has
not been used to augment the state daily incarceration cost in another year, add the rate surplus
- 172 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
or surpluses to the state daily incarceration cost and set the state daily incarceration rate to that
combined amount, up to 105% of the previous year's state daily incarceration rate; and
5445 [(b)] (c) inform each county and the [commission] Department of Criminal Justice of the state daily
incarceration rate.
5447 (2) Except as provided in Subsections (3) and (4), the state daily incarceration rate may not be less that
the rate presented to the Executive Appropriations Committee of the Legislature for purposes of
setting the appropriation for the department's budget.
5450 (3) Notwithstanding any other provision in this section, in a fiscal year where General Fund revenue
growth is not sufficient to fund the state daily incarceration rate presented to the Executive
Appropriations Committee, the state daily incarceration rate shall be reset by the Executive
Appropriations Committee in an appropriations act.
5454 (4) For the fiscal year beginning July 1, 2025, only, the state daily incarceration rate is $120.75.

the criminal justice hearings provided in Subsections 77-38-2(5)(a) through (g);
5525 (j) collect restitution information in preparing a presentence investigation report as described in Section
77-38b-203;
5527 (k) for an individual under supervision by the division who violates a sentencing protective order issued
under Title 78B, Chapter 7, Part 8, Criminal Protective Orders, report the violation to the court and
notify the victim protected by the order of the violation as required by Section 78B-7-807; and
5531 (l) comply with any other requirement established by applicable statute or regulation or a directive from
the executive director.
5533 (2) The division may, in the course of supervising individuals on probation and parole:
5534 (a) respond to an individual's violation of one or more terms of the probation or parole in accordance
with the graduated and evidence-based processes established by the adult sentencing and
supervision length guidelines, as defined in Section [63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101; and
5538
- 175 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(b) upon approval by the court or the Board of Pardons and Parole, impose as a sanction for an
individual's violation of the terms of probation or parole a period of incarceration of not more than
three consecutive days and not more than a total of six days within a period of 30 days.

agreement, including the case action plan.

of supervision for an offender at a rate of up to 30 days for each month that the offender maintains

requesting termination of supervision based on the eligibility criteria in the adult sentencing and

5647 (i) maintain a record of credits earned by an offender under this Subsection (7); and
5648 (ii) request from the court or the Board of Pardons and Parole the termination of probation or parole not

Subsection (7).
5651 (d) This Subsection (7) does not prohibit the division from requesting a termination date earlier than the
termination date established by earned credits under Subsection (7)(c).
5653 (e) The court or the Board of Pardons and Parole shall terminate an offender's probation or parole
upon completion of the period of probation or parole accrued by time served and credits earned
under this Subsection (7) unless the court or the Board of Pardons and Parole finds that termination
would interrupt the completion of a necessary treatment program, in which case the termination of
probation or parole shall occur when the treatment program is completed.
5659 (f) The department shall report annually to the [State Commission on Criminal and Juvenile
Justice] Department of Criminal Justice on or before August 31:
5661 (i) the number of offenders who have earned probation or parole credits under this Subsection (7) in
one or more months of the preceding fiscal year and the percentage of the offenders on probation or
parole during that time that this number represents;
5665 (ii) the average number of credits earned by those offenders who earned credits;
5666 (iii) the number of offenders who earned credits by county of residence while on probation or parole;
5668 (iv) the cost savings associated with sentencing reform programs and practices; and
5669 (v) a description of how the savings will be invested in treatment and early-intervention programs and
practices at the county and state levels.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) The department shall coordinate with a local mental health authority to complete the requirements of
this Subsection (8) for an offender who:
5673 (i) is a habitual offender as that term is defined in Section 77-18-102;
5674 (ii) has a mental illness as that term is defined in Section 26B-5-301; and
