months before termination of an offender's parole or expiration of an offender's sentence, the
department shall coordinate with the Department of Health and Human Services and the relevant
local mental health authority to provide applicable clinical assessments and transitional treatment
planning and services for the offender so that the offender may receive appropriate treatment and
support services after the termination of parole or expiration of sentence.
5685 (c) The local mental health authority may determine whether the offender:
5686 (i) meets the criteria for civil commitment;
5687 (ii) meets the criteria for assisted outpatient treatment; or
5688 (iii) would benefit from assignment to an assertive community treatment team or available community-
based services.
5690 (d) Based on the local mental health authority's determination under Subsection (8)(c), the local mental
health authority shall, as appropriate:
5692 (i) initiate an involuntary commitment court proceeding;
5693 (ii) file a written application for assisted outpatient treatment; or
5694 (iii) seek to have the offender assigned to an assertive community treatment team or available
community-based services.
5696 (e) On or before November 1, 2025, the department shall provide a report to the Law Enforcement and
Criminal Justice Interim Committee regarding any proposed changes to the requirements in this
Subsection (8), including whether the requirements of this Subsection (8) should also apply to any
other category of offenders.
5789 Section 73. Section 64-14-302 is amended to read:
5790 64-14-302. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Adult Probation and Parole
Employment Incentive Program.
- 180 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
5704 (1) There is created the Adult Probation and Parole Employment Incentive Program.
5705 (2) The department and the office shall implement the program in accordance with the requirements of
this chapter.
5707 (3) Beginning July 2026, and each July after 2026, the department shall calculate and report to the
office, for the preceding fiscal year, for each region and statewide:
5709 (a) the parole employment rate and the average length of employment of individuals on parole;
5711 (b) the probation employment rate and average length of employment of individuals on felony
probation;
5713 (c) the recidivism percentage, using applicable recidivism metrics described in Subsections
[63M-7-102(1) and (3)] 75E-2-203(1) and (3);
5715 (d) the number and percentage of individuals who successfully complete parole or felony probation;
5717 (e) if the recidivism percentage described in Subsection (3)(c) represents a decrease in the recidivism
percentage when compared to the fiscal year immediately preceding the fiscal year to which the
recidivism percentage described in Subsection (3)(c) relates, the estimated costs of incarceration
savings to the state, based on the marginal cost of incarceration;
5722 (f) the number of individuals who successfully complete parole and, during the entire six months before
the day on which the individuals' parole ends, held eligible employment; and
5725 (g) the number of individuals who successfully complete felony probation and, during the entire six
months before the day on which the individuals' parole ended, held eligible employment.
5728 (4) In addition to the information described in Subsection (3), the department shall report, for each
region, the number and types of parole or probation programs that were created, replaced, or
discontinued during the preceding fiscal year.
5731 (5) After receiving the information described in Subsections (3) and (4), the office, in consultation with
the department, shall, for each region:
5733 (a) add the region's baseline parole employment rate and the region's baseline probation employment
rate;
5735 (b) add the region's parole employment rate and the region's probation employment rate;
5736 (c) subtract the sum described in Subsection (5)(a) from the sum described in Subsection (5)(b); and
5738 (d)
(i) if the rate difference described in Subsection (5)(c) is zero or less than zero, assign an employment
incentive payment of zero to the region; or
- 181 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
5740 (ii) except as provided in Subsection (7), if the rate difference described in Subsection (5)(c) is greater
than zero, assign an employment incentive payment to the region by:
5743 (A) multiplying the rate difference by the average daily population for that region; and
5745 (B) multiplying the product of the calculation described in Subsection (5)(d)(ii)(A) by $2,500.
5747 (6) In addition to the employment incentive payment described in Subsection (5), after receiving the
information described in Subsections (3) and (4), the office, in consultation with the department,
shall, for each region, multiply the sum of the numbers described in Subsections (3)(f) and (g) for
the region by $2,500 to determine the end-of-supervision employment incentive payment for the
region.
5752 (7) The employment incentive payment, or end-of-supervision employment supervision payment, for a
region is zero if the recidivism percentage for the region, described in Subsection (3)(c), represents
an increase in the recidivism percentage when compared to the fiscal year immediately preceding
the fiscal year to which the recidivism percentage for the region, described in Subsection (3)(c),
relates.
5757 (8) Upon determining an employment incentive payment for a region in accordance with Subsections
(5)(d)(ii), (6), and (7), the office shall authorize distribution, from the restricted account, of the
incentive payment as follows:
5760 (a) 15% of the payment may be used by the department for expenses related to administering the
program; and
5762 (b) 85% of the payment shall be used by the region to improve and expand supervision and
rehabilitative services to individuals on parole or adult probation, including by:
5764 (i) implementing and expanding evidence-based practices for risk and needs assessments for
individuals;
5766 (ii) implementing and expanding intermediate sanctions, including mandatory community service,
home detention, day reporting, restorative justice programs, and furlough programs;
5769 (iii) expanding the availability of evidence-based practices for rehabilitation programs, including drug
and alcohol treatment, mental health treatment, anger management, cognitive behavior programs,
and job training and other employment services;
5773 (iv) hiring additional officers, contractors, or other personnel to implement evidence-based practices for
rehabilitative and vocational programing;
5775
- 182 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(v) purchasing and adopting new technologies or equipment that are relevant to, and enhance,
supervision, rehabilitation, or vocational training; or
5777 (vi) evaluating the effectiveness of rehabilitation and supervision programs and ensuring program
fidelity.
5779 (9)
(a) The report described in Subsections (3) and (4) is a public record.
5780 (b) The department shall maintain a complete and accurate accounting of the payment and use of funds
under this section.
5782 (c) If the money in the restricted account is insufficient to make the full employment incentive
payments or the full end-of-supervision employment incentive payments, the office shall authorize
the payments on a prorated basis.

Interim Committee that contains:
5988 (i) the compiled information received for the current year, if any; and
5989 (ii) the names of any agencies that failed to submit a review that was required under this section.
5991 (4) This section does not apply to the Division of Professional Licensing with regard to offenses in Title
58, Chapters 37 through 37e.
6081 Section 79. Section 75-2-803 is amended to read:
6082 75-2-803. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions -- Effect of homicide on
intestate succession, wills, trusts, joint assets, life insurance, and beneficiary designations --
Petition -- Forfeiture -- Revocation.
- 189 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
5997 (1) As used in this section:
5998 (a) "Conviction" means the same as that term is defined in Section 77-38b-102.
5999 (b) "Decedent" means a deceased individual.
6000 (c) "Disposition or appointment of property" includes a transfer of an item of property or any other
benefit to a beneficiary designated in a governing instrument.
6002 (d)
(i) Except as provided in Subsection (1)(d)(ii), "disqualifying homicide" means any felony homicide
offense described in Title 76, Chapter 5, Offenses Against the Individual, for which the elements are
established by a preponderance of the evidence and by applying the same principles of culpability
and defenses described in [Title 76, Utah Criminal Code] Title 76, Criminal Offenses.
6007 (ii) "Disqualifying homicide" does not include an offense for:
6008 (A) automobile homicide, as described in Section 76-5-207; and
6009 (B) automobile homicide involving using a handheld wireless communication device while driving, as
described in Section 76-5-207.5.
6011 (e) "Governing instrument" means a governing instrument executed by the decedent.
6012 (f) "Killer" means an individual who commits a disqualifying homicide.
6013 (g) "Revocable" means a disposition, appointment, provision, or nomination under which the decedent,
at the time of or immediately before death, was alone empowered, by law or under the governing
instrument, to cancel the designation in favor of the killer regardless of whether at the time or
immediately before death:
6017 (i) the decedent was empowered to designate the decedent in place of the decedent's killer; or
6019 (ii) the decedent had the capacity to exercise the power.
6020 (2)
(a) An individual who commits a disqualifying homicide of the decedent forfeits all benefits under
this chapter with respect to the decedent's estate, including an intestate share, an elective share, an
omitted spouse's or child's share, a homestead allowance, exempt property, and a family allowance.
6024 (b) If the decedent died intestate, the decedent's intestate estate passes as if the killer disclaimed the
killer's intestate share.
6026 (3) The killing of the decedent by means of a disqualifying homicide:
6027 (a) revokes any revocable:
6028 (i) disposition or appointment of property made by the decedent to the killer in a governing instrument;
- 190 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
6030 (ii) provision in a governing instrument conferring a general or nongeneral power of appointment on the
killer; and
6032 (iii) nomination of the killer in a governing instrument, nominating or appointing the killer to serve in
any fiduciary or representative capacity, including a personal representative, executor, trustee, or
agent; and
6035 (b) severs the interests of the decedent and killer in property held by them at the time of the killing as
joint tenants with the right of survivorship, transforming the interests of the decedent and killer into
tenancies in common.
6038 (4) A severance under Subsection (3)(b) does not affect any third-party interest in property acquired
for value and in good faith reliance on an apparent title by survivorship in the killer unless a writing
declaring the severance has been noted, registered, filed, or recorded in records appropriate to
the kind and location of the property which are relied upon, in the ordinary course of transactions
involving such property, as evidence of ownership.
6044 (5) Provisions of a governing instrument are given effect as if the killer disclaimed all provisions
revoked by this section or, in the case of a revoked nomination in a fiduciary or representative
capacity, as if the killer predeceased the decedent.
6047 (6) A wrongful acquisition of property or interest by one who kills another under circumstances not
covered by this section shall be treated in accordance with the principle that a killer cannot profit
from the killer's wrong.
6050 (7)
(a) An interested person may petition the court to determine whether an individual has committed a
disqualifying homicide of the decedent.
6052 (b) An individual has committed a disqualifying homicide of the decedent for purposes of this section
if:
6054 (i) unless the court finds that disinheritance would create a manifest injustice, the court finds that, by
a preponderance of the evidence, the individual has committed a disqualifying homicide of the
decedent; or
6057 (ii) the court finds that a judgment of conviction has been entered against the individual for a
disqualifying homicide of the decedent and all direct appeals for the judgment have been exhausted.
6060 (8)
- 191 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) Before a court determines whether an individual committed a disqualifying homicide of the
decedent under Subsection (7), the decedent's estate may petition the court to:
6063 (i) enter a temporary restraining order, an injunction, or a temporary restraining order and an
injunction, to preserve the property or assets of the killer or the killer's estate;
6066 (ii) require the execution of a trustee's bond under Section 75B-2-702 for the killer's estate;
6068 (iii) establish a constructive trust on any property or assets of the killer or the killer's estate that is
effective from the time the killer's act caused the death of the decedent; or
6071 (iv) take any other action necessary to preserve the property or assets of the killer or the killer's
estate:
6073 (A) until a court makes a determination under Subsection (7); or
6074 (B) for the payment of all damages and judgments for conduct resulting in the disqualifying homicide
of the decedent.
6076 (b) Upon a petition for a temporary restraining order or an injunction under Subsection (8)(a)(i), a
court may enter a temporary restraining order against an owner's property in accordance with Rule
65A of the Utah Rules of Civil Procedure, without notice or opportunity of a hearing, if the court
determines that:
6080 (i) there is a substantial likelihood that the property is, or will be, necessary to satisfy a judgment or
damages owed by the killer for conduct resulting in the disqualifying homicide of the decedent; and
6083 (ii) notice of the hearing would likely result in the property being:
6084 (A) sold, distributed, destroyed, or removed; and
6085 (B) unavailable to satisfy a judgment or damages owed by the killer for conduct resulting in the
disqualifying homicide of the decedent.
6087 (9)
(a)
(i) A payor or other third party is not liable for having made a payment or transferred an item of
property or any other benefit to a beneficiary designated in a governing instrument affected
by a disqualifying homicide, or for having taken any other action in good faith reliance on the
validity of the governing instrument, upon request and satisfactory proof of the decedent's death,
before the payor or other third party received written notice of a claimed forfeiture or revocation
under this section.
6094
- 192 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(ii) A payor or other third party is liable for a payment made or other action taken after the payor or
other third party received written notice of a claimed forfeiture or revocation under this section.
6097 (b)
(i) Written notice of a claimed forfeiture or revocation under Subsection (9)(a) shall be mailed to the
payor's or other third party's main office or home by registered or certified mail, return receipt
requested, or served upon the payor or other third party in the same manner as a summons in a civil
action.
6101 (ii) Upon receipt of written notice of a claimed forfeiture or revocation under this section, a payor or
other third party may pay any amount owed or transfer or deposit any item of property held by the
payor or third party to or with:
6104 (A) the court having jurisdiction of the probate proceedings relating to the decedent's estate; or
6106 (B) if no proceedings have been commenced, the court having jurisdiction of probate proceedings
relating to the decedent's estates located in the county of the decedent's residence.
6109 (iii) The court shall hold the funds or item of property and, upon the court's determination under this
section, shall order disbursement in accordance with the determination.
6112 (iv) Payments, transfers, or deposits made to or with the court discharge the payor or other third party
from all claims for the value of amounts paid to or items of property transferred to or deposited with
the court.
6115 (10)
(a) A person who purchases property for value and without notice, or who receives a payment or other
item of property in partial or full satisfaction of a legally enforceable obligation, is:
6118 (i) not obligated under this section to return the payment, item of property, or benefit; and
6120 (ii) not liable under this section for the amount of the payment or the value of the item of property
or benefit.
6122 (b) Notwithstanding Subsection (10)(a), a person who, not for value, receives a payment, item of
property, or any other benefit to which the person is not entitled under this section is:
6125 (i) obligated to return the payment, item of property, or benefit to the person who is entitled to the
payment, property, or benefit under this section; and
6127 (ii) personally liable for the amount of the payment or the value of the item of property or benefit to the
person who is entitled to the payment, property, or benefit under this section.
6130
- 193 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(c) If this section or any part of this section is preempted by federal law with respect to a payment, an
item of property, or any other benefit covered by this section, a person who, not for value, receives
the payment, item of property, or any other benefit to which the person is not entitled under this
section is:
6134 (i) obligated to return the payment, item of property, or benefit to the person who would have been
entitled to the payment, property, or benefit if this section or part were not preempted; and
6137 (ii) personally liable for the amount of the payment or the value of the item of property or benefit, to the
person who would have been entitled to the payment, property, or benefit if this section or part were
not preempted.

6276 75E-2-201. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions for part.
2. Department Responsibilities
As used in this part:
6190 (1) "Commission" means the Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice created in Section
75E-3-102.
6192 (2) "Desistance" means an individual's abstinence from further criminal activity after a previous
criminal conviction.
6194 (3) "Intervention" means a program, sanction, supervision, or event that may impact recidivism.
- 195 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
6196 (4) "Recidivism" means a return to criminal activity after a previous criminal conviction.
6197 (5) "Recidivism standard metric" means the number of individuals who are returned to prison for a new
conviction within three years after the day on which the individuals were released from prison.

and ongoing stakeholder engagement in identifying the relevant performance measures;

reporting requirements to streamline reporting, reduce redundancies, eliminate inefficiencies, and

with the Division of Juvenile Justice and Youth Services and the Division of Child and Family

6910 (a) the likelihood that the proposal will accomplish the purpose described in Subsection [(1)(b)] (2)(b);
6912 (b) the cost of the proposal; and
6913 (c) the viability and sustainability of the proposal.
6914 [(4)] (5) Subject to Subsection [(2)] (3), the [commission] department may make rules, in accordance
with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act, to establish:
6917 (a) eligibility criteria for a grant;
6918 (b) the form and process for submitting a proposal to the [commission] department for a grant;
6920 (c) the method and formula for determining a grant amount; and
6921 (d) reporting requirements for a grant recipient.

7178
CHAPTER 4. Sentencing Commission
7179
Part 1. General Provisions
7269 [63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions for chapter.
As used in this [part] chapter:
7182 (1) "Adjudication" means an adjudication, as that term is defined in Section 80-1-102, of an offense
under Section 80-6-701.
7184 (2) "Adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines" means the guidelines established in Section
[63M-7-404.3] 75E-4-203.
7186 (3) "Civil disability" means a legal right or privilege that is revoked as a result of the individual's
conviction or adjudication.
7188 (4) "Collateral consequence" means:
7189 (a) a discretionary disqualification; or
7190 (b) a mandatory sanction.
7191 (5) "Commission" means the Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice created in Section
75E-3-102.
7193 [(5)] (6) "Conviction" means the same as that term is defined in Section 77-38b-102.
7194 [(6)] (7) "Disadvantage" means any legal or regulatory restriction that:
7195 (a) is imposed on an individual as a result of the individual's conviction or adjudication; and
7197 (b) is not a civil disability or a legal penalty.
7198 [(7)] (8) "Discretionary disqualification" means a penalty, a civil disability, or a disadvantage that a
court in a civil proceeding, or a federal, state, or local government agency or official, may impose
on an individual as a result of the individual's adjudication or conviction for an offense regardless of
whether the penalty, the civil disability, or the disadvantage is specifically designated as a penalty, a
civil disability, or a disadvantage.
7204 [(8)] (9) "Juvenile" means a minor as that term is defined in Section 80-1-102.
7205
- 225 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
[(9)] (10) "Juvenile disposition guidelines" means the guidelines established in Section
[63M-7-404.5] 75E-4-204.
7207 [(10)] (11) "Mandatory sanction" means a penalty, a civil disability, or a disadvantage that:
7208 (a) is imposed on an individual as a result of the individual's adjudication or conviction for an offense
regardless of whether the penalty, the civil disability, or the disadvantage is specifically designated
as a penalty, a civil disability, or a disadvantage; and
7212 (b) is not included in the judgment for the adjudication or conviction.
7213 [(11) "Master offense list" means a document that contains all offenses that exist in statute and each
offense's associated penalty.]
7215 (12) "Offense" means a felony, a misdemeanor, an infraction, or an adjudication under the laws of this
state, another state, or the United States.
7217 (13) "Penalty" means an administrative, civil, or criminal sanction imposed to punish the individual for
the individual's conviction or adjudication.
7219 (14) "Sentencing commission" means the sentencing commission created in Section
[63M-7-401.2] 75E-4-102.

7376 [(iii) negligently operating a vehicle resulting in death, Section 76-5-207.]
7377 [(b) The guidelines under Subsection (2)(a) shall consider the following:]
7378 [(i) the current sentencing requirements for driving under the influence of alcohol, drugs, or a
combination of both as identified in Section 41-6a-505 when injury or death do not result;]
7381 [(ii) the degree of injury and the number of victims suffering injury or death as a result of the offense;]
7383 [(iii) the offender's number of previous convictions for driving under the influence related offenses as
defined in Subsection 41-6a-501(2)(a); and]
7385 [(iv) whether the offense amounts to extreme DUI, as that term is defined in Section 41-6a-501.]
7387 [(3) On or before October 31, 2024, the sentencing commission shall review and revise the supervision
tools in the adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines to:]
7389 [(a) recommend appropriate sanctions for an individual who violates probation or parole by:]
7391 [(i) committing a felony offense, a misdemeanor offense described in Title 76, Chapter 5, Offenses
Against the Individual, or a misdemeanor offense for driving under the influence described in
Section 41-6a-502;]
7394 [(ii) possessing a dangerous weapon; or]

7398 [(i) completes all conditions of probation or parole; or]
7399 [(ii) maintains eligible employment as defined in Section 64-14-301.]

should consider when determining the period in which a habitual offender, as defined in Section
77-18-102, will be incarcerated.]
7404 [(5) The sentencing commission shall modify:]
7405 [(a) the adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines to reduce recidivism for the purposes of
protecting the public and ensuring efficient use of state funds; and]
7407 [(b) the criminal history score in the adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines to reduce
recidivism, including factors in an offender's criminal history that are relevant to the accurate
determination of an individual's risk of offending again.]

- 231 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
7501 [63M-7-404.5] 75E-4-204. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Juvenile disposition
guidelines.
7413 (1) The sentencing commission shall establish and maintain juvenile disposition guidelines that:
7415 (a) respond to public comment;
7416 (b) relate dispositional practices and rehabilitative resources;
7417 (c) increase [equity] consistency in disposition orders;
7418 (d) better define responsibility for disposition orders; and
7419 (e) enhance the discretion of the juvenile court while preserving the role of the Youth Parole Authority.
7421 (2) The juvenile disposition guidelines shall address how to appropriately respond to negative and
positive behavior of juveniles who are:
7423 (a) nonjudicially adjusted;
7424 (b) placed on diversion;
7425 (c) placed on probation;
7426 (d) placed on community supervision;
7427 (e) placed in an out-of-home placement; or
7428 (f) placed in a secure care facility.
7429 (3) The juvenile disposition guidelines shall include:
7430 (a) other sanctions and incentives including:
7431 (i) recommended responses that are swift and certain;
7432 (ii) a continuum of community-based options for juveniles living at home;
7433 (iii) recommended responses that target the juvenile's criminogenic risk and needs; and
7435 (iv) recommended incentives for compliance, including earned discharge credits; and
7436 (b) a recommendation that, when a juvenile court interacts with a juvenile described in Subsection (2),
the juvenile court shall consider:
7438 (i) the seriousness of the negative and positive behavior of the juvenile;
7439 (ii) the juvenile's conduct postadjudication; and
7440 (iii) the juvenile's delinquency history[; and] .
7441 [(c) appropriate sanctions for a juvenile who commits sexual exploitation of a minor as described
in Section 76-5b-201, or aggravated sexual exploitation of a minor as described in Section
76-5b-201.1, including the application of aggravating and mitigating factors specific to the offense.]

7536 [63M-7-405] 75E-4-205. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Annual offense report --
Collateral consequences guide.
7449 (1)
(a) The sentencing commission shall annually create [a master offense list.] an offense report listing
and briefly summarizing every criminal offense that was created, expanded, enhanced, reduced, or
eliminated during the previous legislative session.
7452 (b) On or before June 30 of each year, the sentencing commission shall:
7453 (i) after the last day of the general legislative session, update the [master offense list] report described in
Subsection (1)(a); and
7455 (ii) present the [updated master offense list] report described in Subsection (1)(a) to the Law
Enforcement and Criminal Justice Interim Committee.
7457 (2)
(a) The sentencing commission shall:
7458 (i) identify any provision of state law, including the Utah Constitution, and any administrative rule
that imposes a collateral consequence;
7460 (ii) prepare and compile a guide that contains all the provisions identified in Subsection (2)(a)(i);
and
7462 (iii) update the guide described in Subsection (2)(a)(ii) annually.
7463 (b) The sentencing commission shall state in the guide described in Subsection (2)(a) that:
7465 (i) the guide has not been enacted into law;
7466 (ii) the guide does not have the force of law;
7467 (iii) the guide is for informational purposes only;
7468 (iv) an error or omission in the guide, or in any reference in the guide:
7469 (A) has no effect on a plea, an adjudication, a conviction, a sentence, or a disposition; and
7471 (B) does not prevent a collateral consequence from being imposed;
7472 (v) any laws or regulations for a county, a municipality, another state, or the United States[,] imposing a
collateral consequence are not included in the guide; and
7474 (vi) the guide does not include any provision of state law or any administrative rule imposing a
collateral consequence that is enacted on or after March 31 of each year.
7477 (c) The sentencing commission shall:
7478
- 233 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(i) place the statements described in Subsection (2)(b) in a prominent place at the beginning of the
guide;[ and]
7480 (ii) periodically update the guide; and
7481 [(ii)] (iii) make the guide available to the public on the sentencing commission's website.
7483 [(d) The sentencing commission shall:]
7484 [(i) present the updated guide described in Subsection (2)(a)(iii) annually to the Law Enforcement and

foundations, corporations, and other businesses, agencies, or individuals;
7835 (i) obtain and utilize the services of other governmental agencies upon request; and
7836 (j) act in any other capacity or perform any other acts necessary for the office to successfully fulfill the
office's statutory duties and objectives.
7838 (2) The director may request assistance from the department, the Commission on Criminal and Juvenile
Justice, the Department of Public Safety, and other state agencies in conducting research or
monitoring victims' programs.

8071 Section 131. Section 75E-5-304 is renumbered and amended to read:
8073 [63M-7-503] 75E-5-304. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Compensation not to

7986 (1)
(a) A [reparations] compensation award may not supplant an order for restitution under Title 77,
Chapter 38b, Crime Victims Restitution Act, or under any other provision of law.
7989 (b) An order for restitution may not be considered readily available as a collateral source for a
compensation award granted under this chapter.
7991 (c) Receipt of a compensation award under this chapter is considered an assignment of the victim's
rights to restitution from the offender.
7993 (2) The court may not reduce an order for restitution based on a [reparations] compensation award.
7995 (3)
(a)
(i) If a victim receives a [reparations] compensation award and the office is assigned the
victim's claim for restitution, or a portion of the victim's claim for restitution, under Section

listing the amounts or estimated future amounts of payments made or anticipated to be made to
or on behalf of the victim.
8000 (ii) The office may provide a notice of restitution to the victim or victim's representative before or
at sentencing.
- 249 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8002 (iii) The office's failure to provide notice under Subsection (3)(a)(i) or (ii) does not invalidate the
imposition of the judgment or an order for restitution if the defendant is given the opportunity to
object and be heard as provided in this [part] chapter.
8006 (b)
(i) Any objection by the defendant to the imposition or amount of restitution under Subsection (3)(a)(i)
shall be:
8008 (A) made at the time of sentencing; or
8009 (B) made in writing within 20 days after the day on which the defendant receives the notice
described in Subsection (3)(a) and filed with the court and a copy mailed to the office.

8013 (iii) After a hearing under Subsection (3)(b)(ii), the court shall:
8014 (A) enter an order for restitution in accordance with Section 77-38b-205; and
8015 (B) identify the office as an assignee for the order for restitution.

8022 (a) enter an order for restitution in accordance with Section 77-38b-205; and

8121 Section 132. Section 75E-5-305 is renumbered and amended to read:
8123 [63M-7-509] 75E-5-305. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Grounds for eligibility.
- 250 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8035 (1) A victim is eligible for a [reparations] compensation award under this [part] chapter if:
8036 (a) the claimant is:
8037 (i) a victim of criminally injurious conduct;
8038 (ii) a dependent of a deceased victim of criminally injurious conduct; or
8039 (iii) a representative acting on behalf of one of the above;
8040 (b)
(i) the criminally injurious conduct occurred in Utah; or
8041 (ii) the victim is a Utah resident who suffers injury or death as a result of criminally injurious conduct
inflicted in a state, territory, or country that does not provide a crime victims' compensation
program;
8044 (c) the application is made in writing in a form that conforms substantially to that prescribed by the
office;
8046 (d) the criminally injurious conduct is reported to a law enforcement officer, in the law enforcement
officer's capacity as a law enforcement officer, or another federal or state investigative agency;
8049 (e) the claimant or victim cooperates with the appropriate law enforcement agencies and prosecuting
attorneys in efforts to apprehend or convict the perpetrator of the alleged offense; and
8052 (f) the criminally injurious conduct occurred after December 31, 1986.
8053 (2) A [reparations] compensation award may be made to a victim regardless of whether any
individual is arrested, prosecuted, or convicted of the criminally injurious conduct giving rise to a
[reparations] compensation claim.
8056 (3)
(a) Notwithstanding the requirements of Subsections (1)(d) and (e), a victim of sexual assault is
not required to report the sexual assault to a law enforcement officer or another federal or state
investigative agency or cooperate with the appropriate law enforcement agencies and prosecuting
attorneys to be eligible for a [reparations] compensation award under this section if:
8061 (i) the victim seeks assistance from an advocacy services provider, a criminal justice[ ] system
victim advocate, or a nongovernment organization victim advocate; and
8063 (ii) the advocacy services provider, the criminal justice system victim advocate, or the
nongovernment organization victim advocate completes a questionnaire, provided by the office,
regarding the sexual assault.
8066
- 251 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(b) Notwithstanding the requirement of Subsection (1)(e), a victim who has suffered strangulation in the
course of interpersonal violence is not required to cooperate with the appropriate law enforcement
agencies and prosecuting attorneys to be eligible for a [reparations] compensation award under this
section if the victim:
8070 (i) reports the strangulation to a law enforcement officer or another federal or state investigative agency
after the strangulation occurs; or
8072 (ii) seeks medical care for the strangulation immediately after the strangulation occurs.
8163 Section 133. Section 75E-5-306 is renumbered and amended to read:
8165 [63M-7-510] 75E-5-306. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Ineligible individuals --
Fraudulent compensation claims -- Penalties.
8078 (1) The following individuals are not eligible to receive a [reparations] compensation award:
8079 (a) an individual who does not meet all of the provisions set forth in Section [63M-7-509] 75E-5-305;
8081 (b) the offender;
8082 (c) an accomplice of the offender;
8083 (d) an individual whose receipt of a [reparations] compensation award would unjustly benefit the
offender, accomplice, or another individual reasonably suspected of participating in the offense;
8086 (e) the victim of a motor vehicle injury who was the owner or operator of the motor vehicle and was not
at the time of the injury in compliance with the state motor vehicle insurance laws;
8089 (f) a convicted offender serving a sentence of imprisonment in any prison or jail or residing in any other
correctional facility;
8091 (g) an individual who is on probation or parole if the circumstances surrounding the offense of which
the individual is a victim is a violation of the individual's probation or parole;
8094 (h) an individual whose injuries are the result of criminally injurious conduct that occurred in a prison,
jail, or another correctional facility while the individual was incarcerated; and
8097 (i) an individual who:
8098 (i) submits a fraudulent claim; or
8099 (ii) misrepresents a material fact in requesting a [reparations] compensation award.
8100 (2)
(a) An individual may not knowingly:
8101 (i) submit a fraudulent claim; or
8102 (ii) misrepresent a material fact in requesting a [reparations] compensation award.
- 252 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8103 (b) A violation of Subsection (2)(a) is:
8104 (i) a class B misdemeanor if:
8105 (A) the individual who violates Subsection (2)(a) does not receive a [reparations] compensation award;
or
8107 (B) the value of the [reparations] compensation award received is less than $500;
8108 (ii) a class A misdemeanor if the value of the [reparations] compensation award received is or exceeds
$500 but is less than $1,500;
8110 (iii) a third degree felony if the value of the [reparations] compensation award received is or exceeds
$1,500 but is less than $5,000; and
8112 (iv) a second degree felony if the value of the [reparations] compensation award received is or exceeds
$5,000.

with county or city attorneys for the prosecution of violations under this section when the attorney
general cannot conveniently prosecute.
8117 (4)

but receives a [reparations] compensation award shall reimburse the fund for the amount of the
[reparations] compensation award.
8120 (b) The office may bring a civil action against a victim who does not reimburse the fund for the amount
of the [reparations] compensation award in accordance with Subsection (4)(a).

source.

the compensation specialist shall apportion the compensation award among the dependents.
8285 (3)
(a) If a compensation specialist determines that a claimant will suffer financial hardship unless an
emergency compensation award is made, and a final compensation award appears likely, the office
may pay an amount to the claimant, to be deducted from the final compensation award or repaid by
and recoverable from the claimant to the extent that the payment exceeds the final compensation
award.
8290 (b) The office may limit emergency compensation awards under Subsection (3)(a) to any amount the
office considers necessary.
8292 (4)
- 258 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (4)(b), a compensation award may not exceed $25,000 including
any compensation award for a secondary victim.
8294 (b) A compensation award for medical expenses resulting from serious bodily injury or substantial
bodily injury may not exceed $50,000.
8296 (5) Unless otherwise requested by the claimant, the office shall pay a compensation award for the
victim before a compensation award for a secondary victim.
8298 (6) The compensation specialist shall determine the priority of payment among multiple secondary
victims on a single compensation claim.

8774 Section 149. Section 75E-6-303 is renumbered and amended to read:
8776 [63M-7-1003] 75E-6-303. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Complaint of violation
of victim rights -- Criminal justice agency policy about complaints.
8689 (1)
(a) When a committee receives a complaint, the committee shall review the complaint to determine
whether the complaint alleges a violation of a victim's rights.
8691 (b) If a complaint alleges a violation of a victim's rights in another judicial district, the committee shall
forward the complaint to the judicial district where the violation allegedly occurred.
8694 (2)
(a) If the committee receives a complaint that does not allege a violation of a victim's rights, the
committee shall send a letter to the victim, or the representative of a victim:
- 270 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8696 (i) explaining that the committee may only address a violation of the victim's rights; and
8698 (ii) describing any other resources that may be available to the victim or the representative of the
victim.
8700 (b) The committee shall send the letter described in Subsection (2)(a) within 30 days after the day on
which the committee receives the complaint.
8702 (3) If the complaint does allege a violation of a victim's rights, the committee shall forward a copy of
the complaint to the person that is the subject of the complaint.
8704 (4) The committee shall schedule a meeting for the committee to review the complaint as soon as
practicable.
8706 (5) If a criminal justice agency investigates a complaint regarding a violation of a victim's rights and the
committee receives a complaint about the same violation, the criminal justice agency shall provide
the criminal justice agency's investigative findings related to the complaint to the committee.
8710 (6) After reviewing the complaint and any findings submitted by a criminal justice agency under
Subsection (5), the committee may:
8712 (a) inform the person of a victim's rights and the obligations required by law;
8713 (b) refer the victim, or the representative of a victim, to other resources in the community; or
8715 (c) inform the victim, or the representative of a victim, of the victim's rights and remedies described in
Title 77, Chapter 37, Victims' Rights, Title 77, Chapter 38, Crime Victims, and Utah Constitution,
Article I, Section 28.
8718 (7) Within 30 days after the day on which the committee meeting is held, the chair of the committee
shall send a letter to the victim, or the representative of a victim, describing any action taken by the
committee.
8721 (8) A criminal justice agency shall establish a policy for addressing a complaint alleging a violation of a
victim's rights.

Membership -- Quorum -- Per diem -- Staff support -- Meetings.
8739 (1) There is created within the [commission] department the Domestic Violence Offender Treatment
Board consisting of the following members:
8741 (a) the executive director of the Department of Corrections, or the executive director's designee;
8743 (b) the executive director of the Department of Health and Human Services, or the executive director's
designee;
8745 (c) one individual who represents a state program that focuses on prevention of injury and domestic
violence appointed by the executive director of the Department of Health and Human Services;
8748 (d) the commissioner of public safety for the Department of Public Safety, or the commissioner's
designee;
8750 (e) the chair of the [Utah ]Victim Services Commission or the chair's designee;
8751 (f) the director of the [Utah ]Office for Victims of Crime, or the director's designee;
8752 (g) the chair of the Board of Pardons and Parole, or the chair's designee;
8753 (h) the director of the Division of Juvenile Justice and Youth Services, or the director's designee;
8755 (i) one individual who represents the Administrative Office of the Courts appointed by the state court
administrator; and
8757 (j) ten individuals appointed by the executive director of the commission, including:
8758 (i) the following four individuals licensed under Title 58, Chapter 60, Mental Health Professional
Practice Act:
8760 (A) a clinical social worker;
8761 (B) a marriage and family therapist;
8762 (C) a professional counselor; and
8763 (D) a psychologist;
8764 (ii) one individual who represents an association of criminal defense attorneys;
- 272 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8765 (iii) one criminal defense attorney who primarily represents indigent criminal defendants;
8767 (iv) one individual who represents an association of prosecuting attorneys;
8768 (v) one individual who represents law enforcement;
8769 (vi) one individual who represents an association of criminal justice victim advocates; and
8771 (vii) one individual who represents a nonprofit organization that provides domestic violence victim
advocate services.
8773 (2)
(a) A member may not serve on the board for more than eight consecutive years.
8774 (b) If a vacancy occurs in the membership of the board appointed under Subsection (1), the member
shall be replaced in the same manner in which the original appointment was made.
8777 (c) A member of the board serves until the member's successor is appointed.
8778 (3) The members of the board shall vote on a chair and co-chair of the board to serve for two years.
8780 (4)
(a) A majority of the board members constitutes a quorum.
8781 (b) The action of a majority of a quorum constitutes an action of the board.
8782 (5) A board member may not receive compensation or benefits for the member's service on the board,
but may receive per diem and reimbursement for travel expenses incurred as a board member at the
rates established by the Division of Finance under:
8785 (a) Sections 63A-3-106 and 63A-3-107; and
8786 (b) rules made by the Division of Finance under Sections 63A-3-106 and 63A-3-107.
8787 (6) The commission shall provide staff support to the board.
8788 (7) The board shall meet at least quarterly on a date the board sets.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01

8917 Section 154. Section 75E-8-101 is renumbered and amended to read:
8830
CHAPTER 8. Prosecutor Conduct Commission
8831
- 274 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
Part 1. General Provisions
8921 [63M-7-1101] 75E-8-101. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions for chapter.
As used in this [part] chapter:
8834 (1) "Commission" means the Prosecutor Conduct Commission created in Section
[63M-7-1102] 75E-8-102.
8836 (2) "Complaint" means:
8837 (a) a written complaint regarding professional misconduct by a prosecuting attorney; or
8838 (b) an allegation based on reliable information received in any form, from any source, that alleges,
or from which a reasonable inference can be drawn that a prosecuting attorney has committed
professional misconduct.
8841 (3) "Employer" means:
8842 (a) except as provided in Subsection (3)(b), the attorney general, a district attorney, a county attorney,
or a municipal attorney who employs the prosecuting attorney; or
8844 (b) the chief executive officer of the political subdivision that employs the prosecuting attorney if the
prosecuting attorney is a district or county attorney or a municipal attorney.
8847 (4) "Investigation" means an inquiry into a complaint.
8848 (5) "Knowingly" means taking an action, or failing to take an action, with the knowledge that the
natural or probable consequences are unambiguously prohibited by a legal obligation or professional
standard.
8851 (6) "Legal obligation" means an obligation imposed by the Utah Constitution, the Constitution of the
United States, a statute, a rule of procedure or evidence, or a local rule.
8854 (7) "Professional misconduct" means conduct committed in the course of a prosecution of a felony
offense, a class A misdemeanor offense, or a class B misdemeanor offense that:
8856 (a) purposefully, knowingly, or recklessly violated a clear and unambiguous legal obligation or
professional standard for a prosecuting attorney; and
8858 (b) impacted, or reasonably could have impacted, the substantive or procedural due process rights of an
individual.
8860 (8) "Professional standard" means a standard of conduct imposed by the Utah Rules of Professional
Conduct.
8862 (9) "Prosecuting attorney" means an attorney who brings a criminal prosecution or delinquency
proceeding on behalf of this state or a county or municipality of this state.
- 275 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8864 (10) "Purposefully" means taking an action, or failing to take an action, in order to obtain a result that is
unambiguously prohibited by a legal obligation or professional standard.
8866 (11) "Recklessly" means the conduct is a gross deviation from the standard of conduct for an
objectively reasonable prosecuting attorney:
8868 (a) after considering the nature and the circumstances of a prosecuting attorney's conduct; and
8870 (b) by taking into account whether the prosecuting attorney knew, or should have known:
8871 (i) based on the prosecuting attorney's experience, of the legal obligation or professional standard; and
8873 (ii) the prosecuting attorney's conduct was substantially likely to violate a legal obligation or
professional standard.

9052 Section 158. Section 75E-8-203 is renumbered and amended to read:
9054 [63M-7-1104] 75E-8-203. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Complaint and
investigation process.
8967 (1)
(a) A prosecuting attorney shall report:
8968 (i) any alleged professional misconduct by another prosecuting attorney to that prosecuting
attorney's employer; and
8970 (ii) any statement by a judge or magistrate alleging that another prosecuting attorney has committed
professional misconduct to that prosecuting attorney's employer.
8972 (b) An employer of a prosecuting attorney shall:
8973 (i) investigate any alleged professional misconduct by a prosecuting attorney; and
8974 (ii) submit a complaint regarding the professional misconduct to the commission if the employer
determines that the allegation is substantiated.
8976 (2) An individual may submit a complaint to the commission alleging that a prosecuting attorney has
committed professional misconduct.
8978 (3) On a motion by a member of the commission, the commission may initiate an investigation of
alleged professional misconduct by a prosecuting attorney if the commission determines that a
complaint, if substantiated, would lead to a finding of professional misconduct by the prosecuting
attorney.
8982 (4)
(a) The commission may dismiss a complaint at any time if the commission determines that the
complaint lacks merit.
8984 (b) If a complaint submitted by an individual is dismissed, the commission shall notify the individual
who submitted the complaint.
8986 (5) The commission may investigate a complaint even if the prosecuting attorney has retired or
resigned.
8988 (6) If the commission moves to initiate an investigation of alleged professional misconduct by a
prosecuting attorney, the commission shall:
8990 (a) notify the prosecuting attorney and the prosecuting attorney's employer of the investigation; and
8992 (b) provide the prosecuting attorney with all information necessary to prepare an adequate response or
defense, including the identity of the complainant.
- 279 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
8994 (7) If the committee dismisses an investigation after notifying the prosecuting attorney as described in
Subsection (4), the commission shall notify the prosecuting attorney of the dismissal.
8997 (8) A prospective employer may inquire of the commission as to whether there is a pending
investigation against a prosecuting attorney.
8999 (9)
(a) In the course of an investigation, the commission may request that the prosecuting attorney testify
before the commission.
9001 (b) The prosecuting attorney's counsel may be present during the prosecuting attorney's testimony.
9003 (c) The prosecuting attorney may present evidence and material relevant to the complaint.
9005 (10) A governmental entity may provide the commission with a record as described in Section
63G-2-206.
9007 (11)
(a) A prosecuting agency, and an employee of a prosecuting agency, shall:
9008 (i) cooperate with the commission in an investigation of a prosecuting attorney; and
9009 (ii) respond truthfully to questions posed during the course of an investigation unless:
9010 (A) the information is privileged or protected by statute or court rule; or
9011 (B) the employee asserts the employee's constitutional right to remain silent.
9012 (b) A prosecuting agency may subject an employee to discipline, including termination, if the employee
refuses to cooperate with an investigation by the commission.
9014 (c) The dismissal or demotion of a career service employee under Subsection (11)(b) is subject to the
requirements of Section 63A-17-306.
9105 Section 159. Section 75E-8-204 is renumbered and amended to read:
9107 [63M-7-1105] 75E-8-204. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Finding of professional
misconduct -- Reporting of finding.
9020 (1)
(a) Upon an investigation under Section [63M-7-1104] 75E-8-203, the commission may make a finding,
by a preponderance of the evidence, that a prosecuting attorney committed professional misconduct.
9023 (b) In determining whether a prosecuting attorney committed professional misconduct under Subsection
(1)(a), the commission may consider an affirmative action of the prosecuting attorney or an action
that the prosecuting attorney failed to take.
9026 (2) If the commission finds that a prosecuting attorney committed professional misconduct:
- 280 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
9027 (a) the commission shall notify:
9028 (i) the prosecuting attorney's employer of the commission's finding;
9029 (ii) the appropriate law enforcement agency of the commission's finding if the professional misconduct
is likely a criminal offense; and
9031 (iii) the Office of Professional Conduct of the commission's finding if the professional misconduct is
likely a violation of the Utah Rules of Professional Conduct; and
9034 (b) the commission may disclose a summary of the commission's investigation and finding.
9036 (3) Any documents disclosed under Subsection (2) shall maintain the same classification under Title
63G, Chapter 2, Government Records Access and Management Act.
9038 (4) The commission may not disclose information or evidence under Subsection (2) that is:
9039 (a) protected from disclosure by court order or a legal privilege; or
9040 (b) given after having been issued a warning based on Garrity v. New Jersey, 385 U.S. 493 (1967).
9042 (5) A finding by the commission that a prosecuting attorney committed professional misconduct may
only be made public if:
9044 (a) a governmental entity with a record of the finding is required to make the record public under Title
63G, Chapter 2, Government Records Access and Management Act;
9047 (b) the Office of Professional Conduct discloses the commission's finding to the public due to a
disciplinary action against the prosecuting attorney as a result of the commission's finding; or
9050 (c) a prosecuting agency brings a criminal prosecution against the prosecuting attorney as a result of the
commission's finding.
9052 (6) The commission may not discipline or sanction a prosecuting attorney for any professional
misconduct.

9159 Section 161. Section 161 is enacted to read:
9162 75E-9-101. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions for chapter.
9. Indigent Defense Commission
1. General Provisions
As used in this chapter:
9075 (1) "Account" means the Indigent Defense Resources Restricted Account created in Section 75E-9-202.
9077 (2) "Child welfare case" means a proceeding under Title 80, Chapter 3, Abuse, Neglect, and
Dependency Proceedings, or Title 80, Chapter 4, Termination and Restoration of Parental Rights.
9080 (3) "Commission" means the Indigent Defense Commission created in Section 75E-9-102.
9081 (4) "Eligible county" means:
9082 (a) a county of the fourth, fifth, or sixth class, as described in Section 17-60-104; or
9083 (b) a county of the third class, as described in Section 17-60-104, if the county of the third class has no
municipality with a population of 100,000 or more.
9085 (5) "Indigent defense resources" means the same as that term is defined in Section 78B-22-102.
9087 (6) "Indigent defense service provider" means the same as that term is defined in Section 78B-22-102.
9089 (7) "Indigent defense services" means the same as that term is defined in Section 78B-22-102.
9091 (8) "Indigent defense system" means the same as that term is defined in Section 78B-22-102.
9092 (9) "Indigent individual" means the same as that term is defined in Section 78B-22-102.
9093 (10) "Minor" means the same as that term is defined in Section 78B-22-102.
9094 (11) "Office" means the Office of Indigent Defense Services created in Section 75E-10-102.

9264 Section 164. Section 75E-9-104 is renumbered and amended to read:
9266 [78B-22-404] 75E-9-104. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Powers and duties of the
commission.
9179 (1) The commission shall:
9180 (a) adopt core principles for an indigent defense system to ensure the effective representation of
indigent individuals consistent with the requirements of the United States Constitution, the Utah
Constitution, and the Utah Code, which principles at a minimum shall address the following:
9184 (i) an indigent defense system shall ensure that in providing indigent defense services:
9185 (A) an indigent individual receives conflict-free indigent defense services; and
9186 (B) there is a separate contract for each type of indigent defense service; and
9187 (ii) an indigent defense system shall ensure an indigent defense service provider has:
9188 (A) the ability to exercise independent judgment without fear of retaliation and is free to represent an
indigent individual based on the indigent defense service provider's own independent judgment;
9191 (B) adequate access to indigent defense resources;
9192 (C) the ability to provide representation to accused individuals in criminal cases at the critical stages
of proceedings, and at all stages to indigent individuals in juvenile delinquency and child welfare
proceedings;
9195 (D) a workload that allows for sufficient time to meet with clients, investigate cases, file appropriate
documents with the courts, and otherwise provide effective assistance of counsel to each client;
9198 (E) adequate compensation without financial disincentives;
9199 (F) appropriate experience or training in the area for which the indigent defense service provider is
representing indigent individuals;
9201 (G) compensation for legal training and education in the areas of the law relevant to the types of cases
for which the indigent defense service provider is representing indigent individuals; and
9204
- 285 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(H) the ability to meet the obligations of the Utah Rules of Professional Conduct, including
expectations on client communications and managing conflicts of interest;
9207 (b) encourage and aid indigent defense systems in the state in the regionalization of indigent defense
services to provide for effective and efficient representation to the indigent individuals;
9210 (c) emphasize the importance of ensuring constitutionally effective indigent defense services;
9212 (d) encourage members of the judiciary to provide input regarding the delivery of indigent defense
services;
9214 (e) oversee individuals and entities involved in providing indigent defense services;
9215 (f) manage county participation in the Indigent Aggravated Murder Defense Fund created in Section

9294 (b)

core principles for the effective representation of indigent individuals; or
9297 (ii) the indigent defense system shall use the grant in an innovative manner that meets the commission's
core principles for the effective representation of indigent individuals.
9300 (4) The commission may revoke a grant if an indigent defense system fails to meet requirements of
the grant or any of the commission's core principles for the effective representation of indigent
individuals.

9508 Section 175. Section 75E-10-302 is renumbered and amended to read:
9510 [78B-22-455] 75E-10-302. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Indigent Inmate Fund.
9422 (1) There is created a custodial fund known as the "Indigent Inmate Fund" to be disbursed by the office
in accordance with contracts entered into under Subsection [78B-22-452(1)(g)] 75E-10-202(1)(g).
9425 (2) Money deposited into this fund shall only be used:
9426 (a) to pay indigent defense services for an indigent inmate who:
9427 (i) is incarcerated in a state prison located in a county of the third, fourth, fifth, or sixth class as
classified under Section 17-60-104;
9429 (ii) is charged with having committed a crime within that state prison; and
9430 (iii) has been appointed counsel in accordance with Section 78B-22-203; and
9431 (b) to cover costs of administering the Indigent Inmate Fund.
9432 (3) The fund consists of:
9433 (a) proceeds received from counties that impose the additional tax levy by ordinance under Subsection
[78B-22-454(4)] 75E-10-303(4), which shall be the total county obligation for payment of costs
listed in Subsection (2) for defense services for indigent inmates;
9437 (b) appropriations made to the fund by the Legislature; and
9438 (c) interest and earnings from the investment of fund money.
9439 (4) Fund money shall be invested by the state treasurer with the earnings and interest accruing to the
fund.
9441 (5)
(a) In any calendar year in which the fund has insufficient funding, or is projected to have insufficient
funding, the commission shall request a supplemental appropriation from the Legislature in the
following general session to provide sufficient funding.
9444 (b) The state shall pay any or all of the reasonable and necessary money to provide sufficient funding
into the [Indigent Inmate Fund] fund.
9446 (6) The fund is capped at $1,000,000.
9447 (7) The office shall notify the contributing counties when the fund approaches $1,000,000 and provide
each county with the amount of the balance in the fund.
9449
- 292 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(8) Upon notification by the office that the fund is near the limit imposed in Subsection (6), the counties
may contribute enough money to enable the fund to reach $1,000,000 and discontinue contributions
until notified by the office that the balance has fallen below $1,000,000, at which time counties that
meet the requirements of Section [78B-22-454] 75E-10-303 shall resume contributions.
9543 Section 176. Section 75E-10-303 is renumbered and amended to read:
9545 [78B-22-454] 75E-10-303. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Funding for indigent
defense services.
9458 (1) The office shall pay for indigent defense services for indigent inmates from the [Indigent Inmate
Fund created in Section 78B-22-455] fund.
9460 (2) A contract under this [part] chapter shall ensure that indigent defense services are provided in a
manner consistent with the core principles described in Section [78B-22-404] 75E-9-104.
9463 (3) The county attorney or district attorney of a county of the third, fourth, fifth, or sixth class shall
function as the prosecuting entity.
9465 (4)
(a) A county of the third, fourth, fifth, or sixth class where a state prison is located may impose an
additional property tax levy by ordinance at .0001 per dollar of taxable value in the county.
9468 (b) If the county governing body imposes the additional property tax levy by ordinance, the
revenue shall be deposited into the [Indigent Inmate Fund] fund as provided in Section
[78B-22-455] 75E-10-302 to fund the purposes of this [part] chapter.
9471 (c) Upon notification that the fund has reached the amount specified in Subsection
[78B-22-455(6)] 75E-10-302(6), a county shall deposit revenue derived from the property tax levy
after the county receives the notice into a county account used exclusively to provide indigent
defense services.
9475 (d) A county that chooses not to impose the additional levy by ordinance may not receive any benefit
from the [Indigent Inmate Fund] fund.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) A county that elects to initiate participation in the fund, or reestablish participation in the fund after

amount becomes the base assessment until another deficit year occurs.
9601 (6) In a calendar year in which the fund runs a deficit, or is projected to run a deficit, the commission
shall request a supplemental appropriation to pay for the deficit from the Legislature in the
following general session.
9604 (7) The state shall pay any or all of the reasonable and necessary money for the deficit into the fund.

9671 (iii) have experience in child welfare cases; and
9672 (iv) participate each calendar year in continuing legal education courses providing no fewer than
eight hours of instruction in child welfare law.
9674 (b) In accordance with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act, the commission
may, by rule, exempt from the requirements of Subsection (2)(a) an attorney who has equivalent
training or adequate experience.

9901 (1) A county may participate in the fund in accordance with the provisions of this section.
9902 (2) A county that does not participate in the fund, or is not current in the county's assessments for the

9905 (3) The commission may revoke a county's participation in the fund if the county fails to pay the
county's assessments when the assessments are due.
9907 (4) To participate in the fund, the legislative body of a county shall:
9908 (a) adopt a resolution that approves participation in the fund and commits the county to fulfilling the
assessment requirements; and
9910 (b) submit a certified copy of that resolution together with an application to the commission.

determined by the office.
9914 (6) A participating county may withdraw from participation in the fund upon:
9915 (a) adoption by the county's legislative body of a resolution to withdraw; and
9916 (b) notice to the commission on or before January 1 of the year in which the county intends to
withdraw.
9918 (7) A county withdrawing from participation in the fund, or whose participation in the fund has been
revoked for failure to pay the county's assessments when the assessments are due, shall forfeit the
right to:
9921 (a) any previously paid assessment;
9922 (b) relief from the county's obligation to pay the county's assessment during the period of the county's
participation in the fund; and
9924 (c) any benefit from the fund, including reimbursement of costs that accrued after the last day of the
period for which the county has paid the county's assessment.
9926 (8)
(a) If the fund runs a deficit during a calendar year, the state is responsible for the deficit.
9928 (b) In the calendar year following a deficit year, the office shall increase the amount of the annual
assessment that is required for participation in the fund by an amount at least equal to the deficit of
the previous calendar year.
9931
- 306 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(c) In a calendar year in which the fund runs a deficit, or is projected to run a deficit, the office shall
request a supplemental appropriation to pay for the deficit from the Legislature in the following
general session.
9934 (d) The state shall pay any or all of the reasonable and necessary money for the deficit into the fund.

1 0131 (ii) while the applicant is enrolled in the program, notify the [commission] department when the

1 0164 (ii) is at risk of discovery by the assailant or potential assailant;

1 0207 (i) an assigned address;

the enrollment is granted, unless the enrollment is withdrawn, or is cancelled under Section
[77-38-617] 75E-11-316, before the end of the four-year period.
1 0215 (3) A program participant may withdraw from the program by filing a request for withdrawal with the
[commission] department that is acknowledged before a notary public.
1 0218 (4)
(a) A program participant may renew enrollment by filing a renewal application with the
[commission] department at least 30 days before the day on which enrollment in the program will
expire.
1 0221 (b) The applicant shall date, sign, and verify the renewal application.
1 0222 (c) The renewal application shall contain:
1 0223 (i) all statements or information required by Subsection [77-38-605(3)] 75E-11-304(3) that have
changed from the original application or a prior renewal application;
1 0225 (ii) a statement by the applicant that the applicant, or a minor or an incapacitated individual residing at
the same address as the applicant, will continue to benefit from participation in the program; and
1 0228 (iii) a statement by the applicant, under penalty of perjury, that the information contained in the renewal
application is true.

1 0518 Section 210. Section 75E-11-311 is renumbered and amended to read:
1 0520 [77-38-612] 75E-11-311. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Request for disclosure.
1 0433 (1) A state or local government entity requesting disclosure of a program participant's actual address in
accordance with this section shall make the request:
1 0435 (a) in writing;
- 321 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0436 (b) on the state or local government entity's letterhead; and
1 0437 (c) with the signature of the head or an executive-level official of the state or local government entity.
1 0439 (2) In accordance with Subsection (1), a state or local government entity requesting disclosure of a
program participant's actual address shall provide the [commission] department with the name of the
program participant and a statement:
1 0442 (a) explaining why the state or local government entity is requesting the program participant's actual
address;
1 0444 (b) explaining why the state or local government entity cannot meet the state or local government
entity's statutory or administrative obligations without the disclosure of the program participant's
actual address;
1 0447 (c) of facts showing that:
1 0448 (i) other methods to locate the program participant's actual address have failed;
1 0449 (ii) other methods will be unlikely to succeed; or
1 0450 (iii) other means of contacting the program participant have failed or are unavailable; and
1 0452 (d) that the state or local government entity has adopted a procedure to protect the confidentiality of the
program participant's actual address.
1 0454 (3) In response to a request for disclosure under Subsection (2), the [commission] department may
request additional information from the state or local government entity to help identify the
program participant in the records of the office or to assess whether disclosure to the state or local
government entity is permitted under this part.
1 0458 (4)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (4)(b), after receiving a request for disclosure from a state or local
government entity under Subsection (1), the [commission] department shall provide a program
participant with written notification:
1 0461 (i) informing the participant of the request, and to the extent possible, of an opportunity to be heard
regarding the request; and
1 0463 (ii) after a decision is made by the [commission] department, whether the request has been granted
or denied.
1 0465 (b) The [commission] department is not required to provide notice of a request for disclosure to a
program participant under Subsection (4)(a) when:
1 0467
- 322 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(i) the request is made by a state or local law enforcement agency conducting a criminal investigation
involving alleged criminal conduct by the program participant; or
1 0470 (ii) providing notice to the program participant would jeopardize an ongoing criminal investigation or
the safety of law enforcement personnel.
1 0472 (5) The [commission] department shall grant a state or local government entity's request for disclosure
and disclose the program participant's actual address if:
1 0474 (a) the state or local government entity has demonstrated a good faith statutory or administrative need
for the actual address;
1 0476 (b) the actual address will be used only for the purpose stated in the request;
1 0477 (c) other methods to locate the program participant or the program participant's actual address have
failed or are unlikely to succeed;
1 0479 (d) other means of contacting the program participant have failed or are unavailable; and
1 0480 (e) the state or local government entity has adopted a procedure to protect the confidentiality of the
program participant's actual address.
1 0482 (6) If the [commission] department grants a request for disclosure under this section, the
[commission] department shall provide the state or local government entity with a disclosure that
contains:
1 0485 (a) the program participant's actual address;
1 0486 (b) a statement of the permitted use of the program participant's actual address;
1 0487 (c) the names or classes of persons permitted to have access to or use of the program participant's actual
address;
1 0489 (d) a statement that the state or local government entity is required to limit access to and use of the
program participant's actual address to the permitted use and to the listed persons or classes of
persons; and
1 0492 (e) if expiration of the disclosure is appropriate, the date on which the permitted use of the program
participant's actual address expires.
1 0494 (7) If a request for disclosure is granted by the [commission] department, a state or local government
entity shall:
1 0496 (a) limit use of the program participant's actual address to the purpose stated in the disclosure;
1 0498 (b) limit access to the program participant's actual address to the persons or classes of persons stated in
the disclosure;
- 323 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0500 (c) cease use of the program participant's actual address upon the expiration of the permitted use;
1 0502 (d) dispose of the program participant's actual address upon the expiration of the permitted use; and
1 0504 (e) except as permitted in the request for disclosure, maintain the confidentiality of the program
participant's actual address.
1 0506 (8) Upon denial of a state or local government entity's request for disclosure, the
[commission] department shall promptly provide a written notification to the state or local
government entity explaining the specific reasons for denying the request for disclosure.
1 0509 (9)
(a) A state or local government entity may file a written appeal with the [commission] department no
later than 15 days after the day on which the state or local government entity receives the written
notification under Subsection (8).
1 0512 (b) A state or local government entity filing a written appeal under Subsection (9)(a) shall:
1 0514 (i) restate the information contained in the request for disclosure; and
1 0515 (ii) respond to the [commission's] department's reason for denying the request for disclosure.
1 0517 (c) The [commission] department shall make a final determination on the appeal within 30 days after
the day on which the appeal is received by the [commission] department, unless the state or local
government entity and the [commission] department agree to a different deadline.
1 0521 (d) Before the [commission] department makes a final determination, the [commission] department may
conduct a hearing or request additional information from the state or local government entity or the
program participant.

1 0641 Section 213. Section 75E-11-314 is renumbered and amended to read:
1 0643 [77-38-615] 75E-11-314. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Participation in the

1 0557 (1) A court may not consider a parent's participation in the program for the purpose of making an order
allocating custody or parent-time under Title 81, Chapter 9, Custody, Parent-time, and Visitation.
1 0560 (2) A court shall take practical measures to keep a program participant's actual address confidential

1 0562 (3) Nothing in this part affects an order relating to the allocation of custody or parent-time in effect
[prior to] before or during a program participant's participation in the program.
1 0652 Section 214. Section 75E-11-315 is renumbered and amended to read:
1 0654 [77-38-616] 75E-11-315. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Disclosure of address or
identifiable information in a judicial or administrative proceeding.
1 0568 (1) A program participant may submit the program participant's actual address to the court as a
safeguarded record in accordance with the Utah Code of Judicial Administration, Rule 4-202.02.
1 0571 (2) A person may not compel disclosure of a program participant's actual address or identifying
information related to the program participant's residence during a proceeding in a court or
administrative proceeding, unless:
- 325 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0574 (a) the court orders the disclosure of the program participant's address; or
1 0575 (b) an administrative tribunal finds, based on a preponderance of the evidence, that:
1 0576 (i) the disclosure is required in the interest of justice;
1 0577 (ii) public interest in the disclosure substantially outweighs the potential harm to the program
participant; or
1 0579 (iii) no other alternative would satisfy the necessity of the disclosure.
1 0580 (3) If disclosure of a program participant's actual address is required in a proceeding before a court or
administrative tribunal, the court or administrative tribunal may safeguard the portion of a record
that contains the program participant's actual address.
1 0583 (4) Nothing in this section prevents a state or local government entity from using a program
participant's actual address in filing a document or record with a court or administrative tribunal if,
at the time of the filing, the document or record is filed under safeguard or not a public record.

1 0593 (a) the program participant submits to the [commission] department a written request to withdraw from
enrollment in accordance with Section [77-38-606] 75E-11-305;
1 0595 (b) the program participant fails to notify the [commission] department of a change in the program
participant's name, actual address, or telephone number that is listed on the application;
1 0598 (c) the program participant, or a parent or guardian of the program participant, knowingly submits false
information in the program application; or
1 0600 (d) mail forwarded to the program participant by the [commission] department is returned as
undeliverable.
1 0602 (2)
(a) If the [commission] department determines that there are grounds for cancelling a program
participant's enrollment in accordance with Subsection (1), the commission shall send notice of the
cancellation with the reason for cancellation to the program participant at the program participant's
actual address and email address.
1 0606 (b) A program participant has 30 days to appeal the cancellation decision in accordance with procedures
developed by the [commission] department.
- 326 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0608 (3) A program participant who receives a notice of cancellation is responsible for notifying a person
who uses the program participant's assigned address to communicate with the program participant
that the assigned address is no longer valid.
1 0611 (4) If the [commission] department cancels a program participant's enrollment in the program, the
program participant is not eligible to participate in the program for six months after the day on
which the [commission] department cancels the program participant's enrollment in the program.
1 0703 Section 216. Section 75E-11-317 is renumbered and amended to read:
1 0705 [77-38-619] 75E-11-317. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Immunity from suit.
1 0618 (1) A program assistant, or a program assistant's employer, is immune from liability in a civil action or
proceeding involving the performance or nonperformance of a duty under this [part] chapter, unless:
1 0621 (a) the performance or nonperformance of a program assistant was manifestly outside the scope of the
program assistant's duties in the program; or
1 0623 (b) the program assistant acted with malicious purpose, bad faith, or in a wanton or reckless manner.
1 0625 (2) In addition to the governmental immunity granted in Title 63G, Chapter 7, Governmental Immunity
Act of Utah, or any other governmental immunity provided by law, the [commission] department,
the state, and the political subdivisions of the state are immune from liability in a civil action or
proceeding involving the performance or nonperformance of a duty under the program.

TITLE 76. Criminal Offenses
