1 0636 (2) Although a provision described in Subsection (1) is not included in non-offense sections in [Title

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
is earlier terminated by the Board of Pardons and Parole in accordance with the adult sentencing

1 0649 (2)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (2)(b), an individual committed to the state prison to serve an

shall, upon completion of three years on parole outside of confinement and without violation, be
terminated from the individual's sentence unless the parole is earlier terminated by the Board of

of a child; or attempting, conspiring, or soliciting the commission of a violation of any of those
sections, and who is paroled before July 1, 2008, shall, upon completion of 10 years parole outside
of confinement and without violation, be terminated from the sentence unless the individual is
earlier terminated by the Board of Pardons and Parole.
1 0668 (4) An individual who violates the terms of parole, while serving parole, for any offense under
Subsection (1), (2), or (3), shall at the discretion of the Board of Pardons and Parole be recommitted
to prison to serve the portion of the balance of the term as determined by the Board of Pardons and
Parole, but not to exceed the maximum term.
1 0672 (5) An individual paroled following a former parole revocation may not be discharged from the
individual's sentence until:
1 0674 (a) the individual has served the applicable period of parole under this section outside of confinement;
1 0676 (b) the individual's maximum sentence has expired; or
1 0677 (c) the Board of Pardons and Parole orders the individual to be discharged from the sentence.
1 0679 (6)
- 328 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) All time served on parole, outside of confinement and without violation, constitutes service toward
the total sentence.
1 0681 (b) Any time an individual spends outside of confinement after commission of a parole violation does
not constitute service toward the total sentence unless the individual is exonerated at a parole
revocation hearing.
1 0684 (c)
(i) Any time an individual spends in confinement awaiting a hearing before the Board of Pardons and
Parole or a decision by the board concerning revocation of parole constitutes service toward the total
sentence.
1 0687 (ii) In the case of exoneration by the board, the time spent is included in computing the total parole
term.
1 0689 (7) When a parolee causes the parolee's absence from the state without authority from the Board of
Pardons and Parole or avoids or evades parole supervision, the period of absence, avoidance, or
evasion tolls the parole period.
1 0692 (8)
(a) While on parole, time spent in confinement outside the state may not be credited toward the service
of any Utah sentence.
1 0694 (b) Time in confinement outside the state or in the custody of any tribal authority or the United States
government for a conviction obtained in another jurisdiction tolls the expiration of the Utah
sentence.
1 0697 (9) This section does not preclude the Board of Pardons and Parole from paroling or discharging
an inmate at any time within the discretion of the Board of Pardons and Parole unless otherwise
specifically provided by law.
1 0700 (10) A parolee sentenced to lifetime parole may petition the Board of Pardons and Parole for
termination of lifetime parole.
1 0790 Section 219. Section 76-5-102.1 is amended to read:
1 0791 76-5-102.1. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Negligently operating a vehicle
resulting in injury.
1 0705 (1)
(a) As used in this section:
1 0706 (i) "Controlled substance" means the same as that term is defined in Section 58-37-2.
- 329 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0707 (ii) "Drug" means the same as that term is defined in Section 76-5-207.
1 0708 (iii) "Negligent" or "negligence" means the same as that term is defined in Section 76-5-207.
1 0710 (iv) "Vehicle" means the same as that term is defined in Section 41-6a-501.
1 0711 (b) Terms defined in Section 76-1-101.5 apply to this section.
1 0712 (2) An actor commits negligently operating a vehicle resulting in injury if the actor:
1 0713 (a)
(i) operates a vehicle in a negligent manner causing bodily injury to another; and
1 0714 (ii)
(A) has sufficient alcohol in the actor's body such that a subsequent chemical test shows that the actor
has a blood or breath alcohol concentration of .05 grams or greater at the time of the test;
1 0717 (B) is under the influence of alcohol, a drug, or the combined influence of alcohol and a drug to a
degree that renders the actor incapable of safely operating a vehicle; or
1 0720 (C) has a blood or breath alcohol concentration of .05 grams or greater at the time of operation; or
1 0722 (b)
(i) operates a vehicle in a criminally negligent manner causing bodily injury to another; and
1 0724 (ii) has in the actor's body any measurable amount of a controlled substance.
1 0725 (3) Except as provided in Subsection (4), a violation of Subsection (2) is:
1 0726 (a)
(i) a class A misdemeanor; or
1 0727 (ii) a third degree felony if the actor has two or more driving under the influence related convictions
under Subsection 41-6a-501(2)(a), each of which is within 10 years of:
1 0730 (A) the current conviction; or
1 0731 (B) the commission of the offense upon which the current conviction is based;
1 0732 (iii) a third degree felony, if the current conviction is at any time after the conviction of:
1 0734 (A) a conviction, as the term conviction is defined in Subsection 41-6a-501(2), that is a felony; or
1 0736 (B) any conviction described in Subsection (3)(a)(iii)(A) for which judgment of conviction is reduced
under Section 76-3-402; or
1 0738 (iv) a third degree felony if the bodily injury is serious bodily injury; and
1 0739 (b) a separate offense for each victim suffering bodily injury as a result of the actor's violation of this
section, regardless of whether the injuries arise from the same episode of driving.
1 0742
- 330 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(4) An actor is not guilty of negligently operating a vehicle resulting in injury under Subsection (2)(b)
if:
1 0744 (a) the controlled substance was obtained under a valid prescription or order, directly from a
practitioner while acting in the course of the practitioner's professional practice, or as otherwise
authorized by Title 58, Occupations and Professions;
1 0747 (b) the controlled substance is 11-nor-9-carboxy-tetrahydrocannabinol; or
1 0748 (c) the actor possessed, in the actor's body, a controlled substance listed in Section 58-37-4.2 if:
1 0750 (i) the actor is the subject of medical research conducted by a holder of a valid license to possess
controlled substances under Section 58-37-6; and
1 0752 (ii) the substance was administered to the actor by the medical researcher.
1 0753 (5)
(a) A judge imposing a sentence under this section may consider:
1 0754 (i) the adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines, as defined in Section
[63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101;
1 0756 (ii) the defendant's history;
1 0757 (iii) the facts of the case;
1 0758 (iv) aggravating and mitigating factors; or
1 0759 (v) any other relevant fact.
1 0760 (b) The judge may not impose a lesser sentence than would be required for a conviction based on the
defendant's history under Section 41-6a-505.
1 0762 (c) The standards for chemical breath analysis under Section 41-6a-515 and the provisions for the
admissibility of chemical test results under Section 41-6a-516 apply to determination and proof of
blood alcohol content under this section.
1 0765 (d) A calculation of blood or breath alcohol concentration under this section shall be made in
accordance with Subsection 41-6a-502(3).
1 0767 (e) Except as provided in Subsection (4), the fact that an actor charged with violating this section is or
has been legally entitled to use alcohol or a drug is not a defense.
1 0769 (f) Evidence of a defendant's blood or breath alcohol content or drug content is admissible except if
prohibited by the Utah Rules of Evidence, the United States Constitution, or the Utah Constitution.
1 0772 (g) In accordance with Subsection 77-2a-3(8), a guilty or no contest plea to an offense described in this
section may not be held in abeyance.
- 331 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0774 (6)
(a) A judge imposing a sentence under this section shall designate the defendant as an interdicted
person, as that term is defined in Section 32B-1-102, for a period of time not to exceed the
probationary period, unless the court finds good cause to order a shorter or longer time.
1 0778 (b) If a court designates a person as an interdicted person as [provided] described in Subsection (6)(a),
the court shall:
1 0780 (i) require the person to surrender the person's identification card or driver license;
1 0781 (ii) notify the Driver License Division that the person is an interdicted person; and
1 0782 (iii) provide the person's identification card or driver license to the Driver License Division.
1 0872 Section 220. Section 76-5-207 is amended to read:
1 0873 76-5-207. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Automobile homicide -- Penalties --
Evidence.
1 0786 (1)

1 0790 (iii) "Drug" means:
1 0791 (A) a controlled substance;
1 0792 (B) a drug as defined in Section 58-37-2; or

impair the ability of an individual to safely operate a vehicle.
1 0795 (iv) "Negligent" or "negligence" means simple negligence, the failure to exercise that degree of care
that reasonable and prudent persons exercise under like or similar circumstances.
1 0798 (v) "Vehicle" means the same as that term is defined in Section 41-6a-501.

1 0836 (v) any other relevant fact.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01

provisions for the admissibility of chemical test results [as provided by] under Section 41-6a-516

1 0852 (6) If, when imposing a sentence under this section, the court finds that it is in the interest of justice to

(2) An actor commits damaging a highway or bridge if the actor intentionally, knowingly, or recklessly

1 0969 Section 222. Section 76-13-211 is amended to read:
1 0970 76-13-211. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Injuring, harassing, or endangering a
service animal.
1 0884 (1)
(a) As used in this section:
1 0885 (i) "Disability" means the same as that term is defined in Section 26B-6-801.
1 0886 (ii) "Search and rescue dog" means a dog:
1 0887 (A) with documented training to locate individuals who are:
1 0888 (I) lost, missing, or injured; or
1 0889 (II) trapped under debris as the result of a natural or man-made event; and
1 0890 (B) affiliated with an established search and rescue dog organization.
1 0891 (iii) "Service animal" means:
1 0892 (A) a service animal as that term is defined in Section 26B-6-801; or
1 0893 (B) a search and rescue dog.
1 0894 (b) Terms defined in Sections 76-1-101.5, 76-13-101, and 76-13-201 apply to this section.
1 0896 (2) An actor commits injuring, harassing, or endangering a service animal if the actor:
1 0897 (a) knowingly, intentionally, or recklessly causes substantial bodily injury or death to a service animal;
1 0899 (b) owns, keeps, harbors, or exercises control over an animal and knowingly, intentionally, or recklessly
fails to exercise sufficient control over the animal to prevent the animal from:
1 0902 (i) causing substantial bodily injury to or the death of a service animal;
1 0903 (ii) causing a service animal's subsequent inability to function as a service animal as a result of the
animal's attacking, chasing, or harassing the service animal; or
1 0905
- 335 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(iii) chasing or harassing a service animal while the service animal is carrying out the service animal's
functions as a service animal, to the extent that the animal temporarily interferes with the service
animal's ability to carry out the service animal's functions; or
1 0909 (c) chases or harasses a service animal.
1 0910 (3)
(a) A violation of Subsection (2)(a), (2)(b)(i), or (2)(b)(ii) is a class A misdemeanor.
1 0911 (b) A violation of Subsection (2)(b)(iii) or (2)(c) is a class B misdemeanor.
1 0912 (4)
(a) A service animal is exempt from quarantine or other animal control ordinances if the service animal
bites an individual while the service animal is subject to an offense under Subsection (2).
1 0915 (b) The owner of the service animal or the individual with a disability whom the service animal serves
shall make the service animal available for examination at a reasonable time and shall notify the
local health officer if the service animal exhibits any abnormal behavior.
1 0919 (5) In addition to any other penalty, an actor convicted of a violation of this section is liable for
restitution to the owner of the service animal or the individual with a disability whom the service
animal serves for the replacement, training, and veterinary costs incurred as a result of the violation
of this section.
1 0923 (6) If the act committed under this section amounts to an offense subject to a greater penalty under
another provision of [Title 76, Utah Criminal Code] this title, than is provided under this section,
this section does not prohibit prosecution and sentencing for the more serious offense.
1 1015 Section 223. Section 77-2-5 is amended to read:
1 0928
TITLE 77. Criminal Procedure
1 1017 77-2-5. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Diversion agreement -- Negotiation --
Contents.
1 0930 (1) At any time after the commencement of prosecution and before conviction, the prosecuting attorney
may, by written agreement with the defendant, filed with the court, and upon approval of the court,
divert a defendant to a non-criminal diversion program.
1 0933 (2) A defendant shall be represented by counsel during negotiations for diversion and at the time of
execution of any diversion agreement unless the defendant has knowingly and intelligently waived
the defendant's right to counsel.
1 0936
- 336 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(3) The defendant has the right to be represented by counsel at any court hearing relating to a diversion
program.
1 0938 (4)
(a) A diversion agreement, entered into between the prosecuting attorney and the defendant and
approved by a court, shall contain a full, detailed statement of the requirements agreed to by the
defendant and the reasons for diversion.
1 0941 (b) The diversion agreement described in Subsection (4)(a) shall include an agreement, by the parties,
for a specific amount of restitution that the defendant will pay, unless the prosecuting attorney
certifies that:
1 0944 (i) the prosecuting attorney has consulted with all victims, including the [Utah ]Office for Victims of
Crime; and
1 0946 (ii) the defendant does not owe any restitution.
1 0947 (5)
(a) If the court approves a diversion agreement that includes an agreement by the parties for the amount
of restitution that the defendant will pay, the court shall order the defendant to pay restitution in
accordance with the terms of the diversion agreement.
1 0951 (b) The court shall collect, receive, process, and distribute payments for restitution to the victim, unless
otherwise provided by law or by the diversion agreement.
1 0953 (6) A decision by a prosecuting attorney not to divert a defendant is not subject to judicial review.
1 0955 (7) A diversion agreement entered into between the prosecution and the defense and approved by a
magistrate may contain an order that the defendant pay a nonrefundable diversion fee that:
1 0958 (a) shall be allocated in the same manner as if paid as a fine for a criminal conviction under Section
78A-5-110 or Section 78A-7-120; and
1 0960 (b) may not exceed the suggested fine listed in the Uniform Fine Schedule adopted by the Judicial
Council.
1 0962 (8) A diversion agreement may not be approved unless the defendant knowingly and intelligently
waives the defendant's constitutional right to a speedy trial before a magistrate and in the diversion
agreement.
1 0965 (9)
(a) The court shall, on the defendant's request, consider the defendant's ability to pay a diversion fee
before ordering the defendant to pay a diversion fee.
- 337 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 0967 (b) The court may:
1 0968 (i) consider any relevant evidence in determining the defendant's ability to pay a diversion fee; and
1 0970 (ii) lower or waive the diversion fee based on that evidence.
1 0971 (10) A diversion program longer than two years is not permitted.
1 0972 (11) The court may not rely solely on an algorithm or a risk assessment tool score in determining
whether the court should approve the defendant's diversion to a non-criminal diversion program.
1 1063 Section 224. Section 77-2a-2 is amended to read:
1 1064 77-2a-2. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Plea in abeyance agreement --
Negotiation -- Contents -- Terms of agreement -- Waiver of time for sentencing.
1 0978 (1) At any time after acceptance of a plea of guilty or no contest but before entry of judgment of
conviction and imposition of sentence, the court may, upon motion of both the prosecuting attorney
and the defendant, hold the plea in abeyance and not enter judgment of conviction against the
defendant nor impose sentence upon the defendant within the time periods contained in Rule 22(a),
Utah Rules of Criminal Procedure.
1 0983 (2) A defendant shall be represented by counsel during negotiations for a plea in abeyance and at the
time of acknowledgment and affirmation of any plea in abeyance agreement unless the defendant
knowingly and intelligently waives the defendant's right to counsel.
1 0986 (3) A defendant has the right to be represented by counsel at any court hearing relating to a plea in
abeyance agreement.
1 0988 (4)
(a) Any plea in abeyance agreement entered into between the prosecution and the defendant and
approved by the court shall, subject to Subsection (7), include a full, detailed recitation of the
requirements and conditions agreed to by the defendant and the reason for requesting the court to
hold the plea in abeyance.
1 0992 (b) If the plea is to a felony or any combination of misdemeanors and felonies, the agreement shall be
in writing and shall, before acceptance by the court, be executed by the prosecuting attorney, the
defendant, and the defendant's counsel in the presence of the court.
1 0996 (5)
(a) Except as provided in Subsection (5)(b), a plea may not be held in abeyance for a period longer than
18 months if the plea is to any class of misdemeanor or longer than three years if the plea is to any
degree of felony or to any combination of misdemeanors and felonies.
- 338 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1000 (b)
(i) For a plea in abeyance agreement that the Division of Adult Probation and Parole created in Section
64-14-202 supervises, the plea may not be held in abeyance for a period longer than the initial term
of probation required under the adult sentencing and supervision length guidelines, as defined in
Section [63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101, if the initial term of probation is shorter than the period required
under Subsection (5)(a).
1 1006 (ii) Subsection (5)(b)(i) does not:
1 1007 (A) apply to a plea that is held in abeyance in a drug court created under Title 78A, Chapter 5, Part 2,
Drug Court, or a problem solving court approved by the Judicial Council; or
1 1010 (B) prohibit court supervision of a plea in abeyance agreement after the day on which the Division of
Adult Probation and Parole supervision described in Subsection (5)(b)(i) ends and before the day on
which the plea in abeyance agreement ends.
1 1014 (6) Notwithstanding Subsection (5), a plea may be held in abeyance for up to two years if the plea
is to any class of misdemeanor and the plea in abeyance agreement includes a condition that the
defendant participate in a problem solving court approved by the Judicial Council.
1 1018 (7) A plea in abeyance agreement may not:
1 1019 (a) be approved unless the defendant, before the court, and any written agreement, knowingly and
intelligently waives time for sentencing as designated in Rule 22(a), Utah Rules of Criminal
Procedure; or
1 1022 (b) notwithstanding any other provision of law, include as part of the requirements and conditions
agreed to by the defendant that the defendant will forfeit a firearm owned by the defendant if the
offense the defendant will plea to is not an offense that would make the defendant a restricted
person under Section 76-11-302[ or] , Section 76-11-303, or federal law.
1 1115 Section 225. Section 77-2a-3 is amended to read:
1 1116 77-2a-3. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Manner of entry of plea -- Powers of
court -- Expungement.
1 1030 (1)
(a) Acceptance of any plea in anticipation of a plea in abeyance agreement shall be done in full
compliance with the Utah Rules of Criminal Procedure, Rule 11.
1 1032 (b) In cases charging offenses for which bail may be forfeited, a plea in abeyance agreement may be
entered into without a personal appearance before a magistrate.
- 339 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1034 (2) A plea in abeyance agreement may provide that the court may, upon finding that the defendant has
successfully completed the terms of the agreement:
1 1036 (a) reduce the degree of the offense, enter a judgment of conviction for the lower degree of the offense,
and impose a sentence for the lower degree of the offense;
1 1038 (b) allow withdrawal of the defendant's plea and order the dismissal of the case; or
1 1039 (c) issue an order of expungement for all records of the offense if:
1 1040 (i) the defendant successfully completes a problem solving court program that is certified by the
Judicial Council; and
1 1042 (ii) the court allows the withdrawal of the defendant's plea and orders the dismissal of the case.
1 1044 (3)
(a) Upon finding that a defendant has successfully completed the terms of a plea in abeyance agreement
and only as provided in the plea in abeyance agreement or as agreed to by all parties, the court may[
]:
1 1047 (i) reduce the degree of the offense, enter a judgment of conviction for the lower degree of the
offense, and impose a sentence for the lower degree of the offense;
1 1049 (ii) allow withdrawal of the defendant's plea and order the dismissal of the case; or
1 1050 (iii) issue an order of expungement for all records of the offense if:
1 1051 (A) the defendant successfully completes a problem solving court program that is certified by the
Judicial Council; and
1 1053 (B) the court allows the withdrawal of the defendant's plea and orders the dismissal of the case.
1 1055 (b) Upon sentencing a defendant for any lesser offense in accordance with a plea in abeyance
agreement, the court may not invoke Section 76-3-402 to further reduce the degree of the offense.
1 1058 (4) The court may require the Division of Adult Probation and Parole created in Section 64-14-202 to
assist in the administration of the plea in abeyance agreement as if the defendant were placed on
probation under Section 77-18-105.
1 1061 (5) The terms of a plea in abeyance agreement may include:
1 1062 (a) an order that the defendant pay a nonrefundable plea in abeyance fee, with a surcharge based on
the amount of the plea in abeyance fee, both of which shall be allocated in the same manner as if
paid as a fine for a criminal conviction under Section 78A-5-110 and a surcharge under Title 51,
Chapter 9, Part 4, Criminal Conviction Surcharge Allocation, and which may not exceed in amount
- 340 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
the maximum fine and surcharge which could have been imposed upon conviction and sentencing
for the same offense;
1 1069 (b) an order that the defendant pay the costs of any remedial or rehabilitative program required by the
terms of the agreement; and
1 1071 (c) an order that the defendant comply with any other conditions that could have been imposed as
conditions of probation upon conviction and sentencing for the same offense.
1 1074 (6)
(a) The terms of a plea in abeyance shall include:
1 1075 (i) a specific amount of restitution that the defendant will pay, as agreed to by the defendant and the
prosecuting attorney;
1 1077 (ii) a certification from the prosecuting attorney that:
1 1078 (A) the prosecuting attorney has consulted with all victims, including the [Utah ]Office for Victims of
Crime; and
1 1080 (B) all victims, including the [Utah ]Office for Victims of Crime, are not seeking restitution; or
1 1082 (iii) an agreement between the parties that restitution will be determined by the court at a
subsequent hearing in accordance with Section 77-38b-205.
1 1084 (b) At a subsequent hearing described in Subsection (6)(a)(iii), the court shall order the defendant, as
a modified term of the plea in abeyance, to pay restitution to all victims for the entire amount of
pecuniary damages that are proximately caused by the criminal conduct of the defendant.
1 1088 (c) The court shall collect, receive, process, and distribute payments for restitution to the victim, unless
otherwise provided by law or by the plea in abeyance agreement.
1 1090 (d) If the defendant does not successfully complete the terms of the plea in abeyance, the court shall
enter an order for restitution, in accordance with Chapter 38b, Crime Victims Restitution Act, upon
entering a sentence for the defendant.
1 1093 (7)
(a) A court may not hold a plea in abeyance without the consent of both the prosecuting attorney and
the defendant.
1 1095 (b) A decision by a prosecuting attorney not to agree to a plea in abeyance is final.
1 1096 (8) No plea may be held in abeyance in any case involving:
1 1097 (a) a sexual offense against an individual who is under 14 years old; or
1 1098
- 341 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(b) a driving under the influence violation under Section 41-6a-502, 41-6a-502.5, 41-6a-517, 41-6a-520,
41-6a-520.1, 41-6a-521.1, 76-5-102.1, or 76-5-207.
1 1100 (9)
(a) If the terms of a plea in abeyance agreement allow a court to issue an order of expungement as
described in Subsection (2)(c), the prosecuting attorney shall make a reasonable effort to provide
notice to any victim of the offense of the terms of the plea in abeyance agreement.
1 1104 (b) The notice under Subsection (9)(a) shall:
1 1105 (i) state that the victim has a right to object to the expungement; and
1 1106 (ii) provide instructions for registering an objection with the court.
1 1107 (c) If there is a victim of the offense, the victim may file an objection with the court before the court
makes a finding as to whether the defendant successfully completed the terms of the plea in
abeyance agreement as described in Subsection (3).
1 1110 (d) The defendant may respond, in writing, to any objection filed by the victim within 14 days after the
day on which the objection is received by the court.
1 1112 (10) If the court issues an order of expungement under Subsection (3)(a)(iii), the court shall:
1 1113 (a) expunge all records of the case as described in Section 77-40a-401; and
1 1114 (b) notify the Bureau of Criminal Identification of the order of expungement.
1 1115 (11)

the Bureau of Criminal Identification shall notify any agency, as defined in Section 77-40a-101,
affected by the expungement order.
1 1118 (b) For purposes of Subsection (11)(a), the Bureau of Criminal Identification may not notify the Board
of Pardons and Parole of an expungement order if the individual has never been:
1 1121 (i) sentenced to prison in this state; or
1 1122 (ii) under the jurisdiction of the Board of Pardons and Parole.
1 1123 (c) The Bureau of Criminal Identification shall forward a copy of the expungement order to the Federal
Bureau of Investigation.
1 1125 (12) The defendant may deliver copies of the expungement to any agency, as defined in Section
77-40a-101, affected by the order of expungement.
1 1127 (13) If an agency receives an expungement order under this part, the agency shall expunge all records
for the case in accordance with Section 77-40a-401.

1 1218 77-7-8.5. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Use of tactical groups -- Reporting
requirements.
1 1131 (1) As used in this section:
1 1132 (a)
(i) "Reportable incident" means:
1 1133 (A) the deployment of a tactical group; or
1 1134 (B) law enforcement officers who serve a search warrant after using forcible entry.
1 1135 (ii) "Reportable incident" does not mean a forced cell entry at a corrections facility.
1 1136 (b) "Tactical group" means a special unit, within a law enforcement agency, specifically trained and
equipped to respond to critical, high-risk situations.
1 1138 (2) On and after January 1, 2015, every state, county, municipal, or other law enforcement agency
shall annually on or before April 30 report to the [Commission on Criminal and Juvenile
Justice] Department of Criminal Justice the following information for the previous calendar year:
1 1142 (a) whether the law enforcement agency conducted one or more reportable incidents;
1 1143 (b) the following information regarding each reportable incident:
1 1144 (i) the organizational title of the agency, task force, or tactical group deployed;
1 1145 (ii) the city, county, and zip code of the location where the reportable incident occurred;
1 1147 (iii) the reason for the deployment;
1 1148 (iv) the type of warrant obtained, if any;
1 1149 (v) if a threat assessment was completed;
1 1150 (vi) if a warrant was obtained, the name of the judge or magistrate who authorized the warrant;
1 1152 (vii) the number of arrests made, if any;
1 1153 (viii) if any evidence was seized;
1 1154 (ix) if any property was seized, other than property that was seized as evidence;
1 1155 (x) if a forcible entry was made;
1 1156 (xi) if a firearm was discharged by a law enforcement officer, and, if so, approximately how many shots
were fired by each officer;
1 1158 (xii) if a weapon was brandished by a person other than the law enforcement officers;
1 1159 (xiii) if a weapon was used by a person against the law enforcement officers and, if a firearm was used,
the number or approximate number of shots fired by the person;

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1161 (xiv) the identity of any law enforcement agencies that participated or provided resources for the
deployment;
1 1163 (xv) if a person or domestic animal was injured or killed by a law enforcement officer; and
1 1165 (xvi) if a law enforcement officer was injured or killed; and
1 1166 (c) the number of arrest warrants served that required a forced entry as provided by Section 77-7-8 and
were not served in conjunction with a search warrant that resulted in a reportable incident.
1 1169 (3) If a warrant is served by a multijurisdictional team of law enforcement officers, the reporting
requirement in this section shall be the responsibility of the commanding agency or governing
authority of the multijurisdictional team.

1 1194 (iv) each law enforcement agency.

open.utah.gov, before August 15 of each year.

1 1295 Section 227. Section 77-7-17.5 is amended to read:
1 1296 77-7-17.5. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Physical body cavity search policy --
Requirements.
1 1210 (1) As used in this section:
1 1211 (a) "Arrestee" means an individual who is in the custody of law enforcement for an offense for which
the individual has not been convicted.
1 1213 (b)
(i) "Body cavity" includes the anus, rectum, vagina, esophagus, or stomach.
1 1214 (ii) "Body cavity" does not include the mouth, ear canal, or nasal passages.
1 1215 (c)
(i) "Physical body cavity search" means a search of a body cavity of an individual that involves
touching the individual with:
1 1217 (A) any part of another individual's body; or
1 1218 (B) an instrument or other item.
1 1219 (ii) "Physical body cavity search" does not include a clothed, pat down search.
1 1220 (2) Each county jail shall adopt and implement a policy that meets the minimum standards contained
in a model policy established by the [Commission on Criminal and Juvenile Justice] Department of
Criminal Justice.
1 1223 (3) The model policy shall specify the minimum standards and procedures to be followed by the county
jail when a body cavity search is performed on an arrestee within the county jail's jurisdiction,
including:
1 1226
- 345 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) stating with specificity the circumstances under which a body cavity search may be performed on an
arrestee;
1 1228 (b) designating who may authorize the performance of a body cavity search;
1 1229 (c) designating specific jail staff or medical personnel who may perform a body cavity search;
1 1231 (d) requiring any nonmedically trained jail staff who may perform a body cavity search to be trained on
safe practices for conducting a body cavity search;
1 1233 (e) requiring documentation of each body cavity search performed at the correctional facility, including:
1 1235 (i) the identity of the arrestee searched;
1 1236 (ii) the date, time, and location of the search;
1 1237 (iii) the identity of the individual performing the search;
1 1238 (iv) the identity of the individual authorizing the search;
1 1239 (v) a description of the body areas searched and the procedures followed in performing the search; and
1 1241 (vi) the circumstances necessitating the body cavity search; and
1 1242 (f) designating rules and procedures to be followed, by authorized staff, when performing a body cavity
search that account for the health and privacy interests of the arrestee, including:
1 1245 (i) the location where a body cavity search must be performed;
1 1246 (ii) the gender requirements of the individuals who perform or observe the search in relation to the
gender of the arrestee being searched; and
1 1248 (iii) methods to ensure the body cavity search is conducted with the minimal amount of touching
necessary to effectuate the purposes of the search.
1 1250 (4) A county jail's body cavity search policy is a public record.

1 1408 Section 230. Section 77-11b-401 is amended to read:
1 1409 77-11b-401. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Disposition and allocation of forfeited
property.
- 348 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1322 (1) If a court finds that property is forfeited under this chapter, the court shall order the property
forfeited to the state.
1 1324 (2)
(a) If the property is not currency, the agency shall authorize a public or otherwise commercially
reasonable sale of that property if the property is not required by law to be destroyed and is not
harmful to the public.
1 1327 (b) If the property forfeited is an alcoholic product as defined in Section 32B-1-102, the property shall
be disposed of as follows:
1 1329 (i) an alcoholic product shall be sold if the alcoholic product is:
1 1330 (A) unadulterated, pure, and free from any crude, unrectified, or impure form of ethylic alcohol, or any
other deleterious substance or liquid; and
1 1332 (B) otherwise in saleable condition; or
1 1333 (ii) an alcoholic product and the alcoholic product's package shall be destroyed if the alcoholic product
is impure, adulterated, or otherwise unfit for sale.
1 1335 (c) If the property forfeited is a cigarette or other tobacco product as defined in Section 59-14-102, the
property shall be destroyed, except that the lawful holder of the trademark rights in the cigarette or
tobacco product brand is permitted to inspect the cigarette before the destruction of the cigarette or
tobacco product.
1 1339 (d) The proceeds of the sale of forfeited property shall remain segregated from other property,
equipment, or assets of the agency until transferred in accordance with this chapter.
1 1342 (3) Before transferring currency and the proceeds or revenue from the sale of the property in accordance
with this chapter, the agency shall:
1 1344 (a) deduct the agency's direct costs, expense of reporting under Section 77-11b-404, and expense of
obtaining and maintaining the property pending a forfeiture proceeding; and
1 1347 (b) if the prosecuting agency that employed the prosecuting attorney has met the requirements of
Subsection 77-11b-105(3), pay the prosecuting attorney the legal costs associated with the litigation
of the forfeiture proceeding, and up to 20% of the value of the forfeited property in attorney fees.
1 1351 (4) If the forfeiture arises from a violation relating to wildlife resources, the agency shall deposit any
remaining currency and the proceeds or revenue from the sale of the property into the Wildlife
Resources Account created in Section 23A-3-201.

Account created in Section 77-11b-402.

1 1455 Section 232. Section 77-11b-403 is amended to read:
1 1456 77-11b-403. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)State Asset Forfeiture Grant
Program.
1 1369 (1) There is created the State Asset Forfeiture Grant Program.
1 1370 (2) The program shall fund crime prevention, crime victim [reparations] compensation, and law
enforcement activities that have the purpose of:
1 1372 (a) deterring crime by depriving criminals of the profits and proceeds of [their ]illegal activities;
1 1374 (b) weakening criminal enterprises by removing the instrumentalities of crime;
1 1375 (c) reducing crimes involving substance abuse by supporting the creation, administration, or operation
of drug court programs throughout the state;
1 1377 (d) encouraging cooperation between agencies;
1 1378 (e) allowing the costs and expenses of law enforcement to be defrayed by the forfeited proceeds of
crime;
1 1380 (f) increasing the equitability and accountability of the use of forfeited property used to assist agencies
in reducing and preventing crime; and
1 1382
- 350 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(g) providing aid to victims of criminally injurious conduct, as defined in Section
[63M-7-502] 75E-5-101, who may be eligible for assistance under [Title 63M, Chapter 7, Part 5,
Utah Office for Victims of Crime] Title 75E, Chapter 5, Office for Victims of Crime.
1 1386 (3)
(a) Upon appropriation of funds from the Criminal Forfeiture Restricted Account, the
[commission] department shall allocate and administer grants to an agency or political subdivision
of the state in compliance with this section and Subsection 77-11b-105(2) and to further the program
purposes under Subsection (2).
1 1390 (b) The [commission] department may retain up to 3% of the annual appropriation from the
Criminal Forfeiture Restricted Account to pay for administrative costs incurred by the
[commission] department, including salary and benefits, equipment, supplies, or travel costs that are
directly related to the administration of the program.
1 1394 (4) An agency or political subdivision shall apply for an award from the program by completing and
submitting forms specified by the [commission] department.
1 1396 (5) In granting the awards, the [commission] department shall ensure that the amount of each award
takes into consideration the:
1 1398 (a) demonstrated needs of the agency or political subdivision;
1 1399 (b) demonstrated ability of the agency or political subdivision to appropriately use the award;
1 1401 (c) degree to which the agency's or political subdivision's need is offset through the agency's or political
subdivision's participation in federal equitable sharing or through other federal and state grant
programs; and
1 1404 (d) agency's or political subdivision's cooperation with other state and local agencies and task forces.
1 1406 (6) The [commission] department may award a grant to any agency or political subdivision engaged in
activities associated with Subsection (2) even if the agency has not contributed to the fund.
1 1409 (7) An applying agency or political subdivision shall demonstrate compliance with all reporting and
policy requirements applicable under this chapter and under [Title 63M, Chapter 7, Criminal Justice
and Substance Abuse] Title 75E, Criminal and Juvenile Justice Administration, in order to qualify as
a potential award recipient.
1 1413 (8)
(a) A recipient agency may only use award money after approval by the agency's legislative body.
1 1415 (b) The award money is nonlapsing.
- 351 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1416 (9) A recipient agency or political subdivision shall use an award:
1 1417 (a) only for law enforcement purposes described in this section, or for victim
[reparations] compensation as described in Subsection (2)(g); and
1 1419 (b) for the purposes specified by the agency or political subdivision in the agency's or political
subdivision's application for the award.
1 1421 (10) A permissible law enforcement purpose for which award money may be used includes:
1 1422 (a) controlled substance interdiction and enforcement activities;
1 1423 (b) drug court programs;
1 1424 (c) activities calculated to enhance future law enforcement investigations;
1 1425 (d) law enforcement training that includes:
1 1426 (i) implementation of the Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution and Utah Constitution,
Article I, Section 7, and that addresses the protection of the individual's right of due process;
1 1429 (ii) protection of the rights of innocent property holders; and
1 1430 (iii) the Tenth Amendment to the United States Constitution regarding states' sovereignty and the states'
reserved rights;
1 1432 (e) law enforcement or detention facilities;
1 1433 (f) law enforcement operations or equipment that are not routine costs or operational expenses;
1 1435 (g) drug, gang, or crime prevention education programs that are sponsored in whole or in part by the
law enforcement agency or its legislative body;
1 1437 (h) matching funds for other state or federal law enforcement grants; and
1 1438 (i) the payment of legal costs, attorney fees, and postjudgment interest in forfeiture actions.
1 1440 (11) A law enforcement purpose for which award money may not be granted or used includes:
1 1442 (a) payment of salaries, retirement benefits, or bonuses to any individual;
1 1443 (b) payment of expenses not related to law enforcement;
1 1444 (c) uses not specified in the agency's award application;
1 1445 (d) uses not approved by the agency's legislative body;
1 1446 (e) payments, transfers, or pass-through funding to an entity other than an agency; or
1 1447 (f) uses, payments, or expenses that are not within the scope of the agency's functions.

1 1646 Section 235. Section 77-18-105 is amended to read:

1 1567 (a) shall impose a sentence in accordance with Section 76-3-201; and
1 1568 (b) subject to Subsection (5), may suspend the execution of the sentence and place the defendant:
1 1570 (i) on probation under the supervision of the division;
1 1571 (ii) on probation under the supervision of an agency of a local government or a private organization; or
1 1573 (iii) on court probation under the jurisdiction of the sentencing court.
1 1574 (3)
(a) The legal custody of all probationers under the supervision of the division is with the department.
1 1576 (b) The legal custody of all probationers under the jurisdiction of the sentencing court is vested as
ordered by the court.
1 1578 (c) The court has continuing jurisdiction over all probationers.
1 1579 (4)
(a) Court probation may include an administrative level of services, including notification to the
sentencing court of scheduled periodic reviews of the probationer's compliance with conditions.

1 1589 (5)
(a) Before ordering supervised probation, the court shall consider the supervision costs to the defendant
for each entity that can supervise the defendant.
1 1591 (b)
(i) A court may order an agency of a local government to supervise the probation for an individual
convicted of any crime if:
1 1593 (A) the agency has the capacity to supervise the individual; and
1 1594 (B) the individual's supervision needs will be met by the agency.

1 1596 (A) the division to supervise the probation for an individual convicted of a class A misdemeanor or any
felony; or
1 1598 (B) a private organization to supervise the probation for an individual convicted of a class A, B, or C

1 1600 (c) A court may not order a specific private organization to supervise an individual unless there is only
one private organization that can provide the specific supervision services required to meet the
individual's supervision needs.
1 1603 (6)
(a) If a defendant is placed on probation, the court may order the defendant as a condition of the
defendant's probation:
1 1605 (i) to provide for the support of persons for whose support the defendant is legally liable;

department, after considering any recommendation by the court as to which jail the court finds

1 1620 (vii) to pay for the costs of investigation, probation, or treatment services;
1 1621 (viii) to pay restitution to a victim with interest in accordance with Chapter 38b, Crime Victims
Restitution Act; or
1 1623 (ix) to comply with other terms and conditions the court considers appropriate to ensure public
safety or increase a defendant's likelihood of success on probation.

a period of time that is served in a county jail immediately before the termination of probation as
long as that period of time does not exceed one year.

one-year limitation described in Subsection (6)(a)(iv) or (6)(c)(i) does not apply to the period of
time that the court orders the defendant to serve in a county jail under this Subsection (6)(c)(ii).

1 1669 (d)

may require a defendant to show cause as to why the defendant's failure to pay in accordance with
the payment schedule should not be treated as contempt of court.
1 1673 (ii) A court may hold a defendant in contempt for failure to make payments for a criminal accounts
receivable in accordance with Title 78B, Chapter 6, Part 3, Contempt.

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) The court may modify the defendant's probation in accordance with the adult sentencing and

1 1707 (b) The court may not:

1 1709 (A) waiver of a hearing by the defendant; or
1 1710 (B) a hearing and a finding by the court that the defendant has violated the terms of probation;

1 1720 (c) The court may find under Subsection (2)(b)(iii)(B) that the defendant does not owe restitution if no
request for restitution has been filed with the court.
1 1722 (3)
(a) Upon the filing of an affidavit, or an unsworn written declaration executed in substantial compliance
with Title 78B, Chapter 18a, Uniform Unsworn Declarations Act, alleging with particularity facts
asserted to constitute violation of the terms of a defendant's probation, the court shall determine if
the affidavit or unsworn written declaration establishes probable cause to believe that revocation,
modification, or extension of the defendant's probation is justified.
1 1728 (b)
(i) If the court determines there is probable cause, the court shall order that the defendant be served
with:
1 1730 (A) a warrant for the defendant's arrest or a copy of the affidavit or unsworn written declaration;
and
1 1732 (B) an order to show cause as to why the defendant's probation should not be revoked, modified, or
extended.
1 1734 (ii) The order under Subsection (3)(b)(i)(B) shall:
1 1735 (A) be served upon the defendant at least five days before the day on which the hearing is held;
1 1737 (B) specify the time and place of the hearing; and
1 1738
- 361 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(C) inform the defendant of the right to be represented by counsel at the hearing, the right to have
counsel appointed if the defendant is indigent, and the right to present evidence at the hearing.
1 1741 (iii) The defendant shall show good cause for a continuance of the hearing.
1 1742 (c) At the hearing, the defendant shall admit or deny the allegations of the affidavit or unsworn written
declaration.
1 1744 (d)
(i) If the defendant denies the allegations of the affidavit or unsworn written declaration, the
prosecuting attorney shall present evidence on the allegations.
1 1746 (ii) If the affidavit, or unsworn written declaration, alleges that a defendant is delinquent, or in default,
on a criminal accounts receivable, the prosecuting attorney shall present evidence to establish, by a
preponderance of the evidence, that the defendant:
1 1750 (A) was aware of the defendant's obligation to pay the balance of the criminal accounts receivable;
1 1752 (B) failed to pay on the balance of the criminal accounts receivable as ordered by the court; and
1 1754 (C) had the ability to make a payment on the balance of the criminal accounts receivable if the
defendant opposes an order to show cause, in writing, and presents evidence that the defendant was
unable to make a payment on the balance of the criminal accounts receivable.
1 1758 (e) The [persons] individuals who have given adverse information on which the allegations are based
shall be presented as witnesses subject to questioning by the defendant, unless the court for good
cause otherwise orders.
1 1761 (f) At the hearing, the defendant may:
1 1762 (i) call witnesses;
1 1763 (ii) appear and speak [in] on the defendant's own behalf; and
1 1764 (iii) present evidence.
1 1765 (g)
(i) After the hearing, the court shall make findings of fact.
1 1766 (ii) Upon a finding that the defendant violated the terms of the defendant's probation, the court may
order the defendant's probation terminated, revoked, modified, continued, or reinstated for all or a
portion of the original term of probation.
1 1769 (4)
(a)
- 362 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(i) Except as provided in Subsection 77-18-105(7), the court may not require a defendant to remain
on probation for a period of time that exceeds the length of the defendant's maximum sentence.
1 1772 (ii) Except as provided in Subsection 77-18-105(7), if a defendant's probation is revoked and later
reinstated, the total time of all periods of probation that the defendant serves, in relation to the
same sentence, may not exceed the defendant's maximum sentence.
1 1776 (b) If the court orders a sanction for a defendant who violated terms of probation, the court may:
1 1778 (i) order a period of incarceration that is consistent with the adult sentencing and supervision length
guidelines, as defined in Section [63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101;
1 1780 (ii) order a period of incarceration that deviates from the guidelines with an explanation for the
deviation on the record;
1 1782 (iii) order treatment services that are immediately available in the community for a defendant that needs
substance abuse or mental health treatment, as determined by a screening and assessment;
1 1785 (iv) execute the sentence previously imposed; or
1 1786 (v) order any other appropriate sanction.
1 1787 (c) If the defendant had, before the imposition of a term of incarceration or the execution of the
previously imposed sentence under this section, served time in jail as a term of probation or due to
a violation of probation, the time that the defendant served in jail constitutes service of time toward
the sentence previously imposed.
1 1791 (5)
(a) Any time served by a defendant:
1 1792 (i) outside of confinement after having been charged with a probation violation, and before a
hearing to revoke probation, does not constitute service of time toward the total probation term,
unless the defendant is exonerated at a hearing to revoke the defendant's probation;
1 1796 (ii) in confinement awaiting a hearing or a decision concerning revocation of the defendant's
probation does not constitute service of time toward the total probation term, unless the
defendant is exonerated at the hearing to revoke probation; or
1 1800 (iii) in confinement awaiting a hearing or a decision concerning revocation of the defendant's
probation constitutes service of time toward a term of incarceration imposed as a result of the
revocation of probation or a graduated and evidence-based response imposed under the adult
sentencing and supervision length guidelines, as defined in Section [63M-7-401.1] 75E-4-101.
1 1805 (b) The running of the probation period is tolled upon:

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1806 (i) the filing of a report with the court alleging a violation of the terms of the defendant's probation; or
1 1808 (ii) the issuance of an order or a warrant under Subsection (3).

1 1957 Section 238. Section 77-20-403 is amended to read:
1 1958 77-20-403. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Disposition of forfeited monetary bail.

- 365 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
forfeited and the forfeiture is not discharged or remitted, the clerk with whom the money is

1 1887 (ii) 25% of the forfeited amount shall be paid to the General Fund.
1 1976 Section 239. Section 77-22-2.5 is amended to read:
1 1977 77-22-2.5. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Court orders for criminal
investigations for records concerning an electronic communications system or service or remote
computing service -- Content -- Fee for providing information.
1 1892 (1) As used in this section:
1 1893 (a)
(i) "Electronic communication" means any transfer of signs, signals, writing, images, sounds, data,
or intelligence of any nature transmitted in whole or in part by a wire, radio, electromagnetic,
photoelectronic, or photooptical system.
1 1896 (ii) "Electronic communication" does not include:
1 1897 (A) a wire or oral communication;
1 1898 (B) a communication made through a tone-only paging device;
1 1899 (C) a communication from a tracking device; or
1 1900 (D) electronic funds transfer information stored by a financial institution in a communications system
used for the electronic storage and transfer of funds.
- 366 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1902 (b) "Electronic communications service" means a service which provides for users the ability to send or
receive wire or electronic communications.
1 1904 (c) "Electronic communications system" means a wire, radio, electromagnetic, photooptical, or
photoelectronic facilities for the transmission of wire or electronic communications, and a computer
facilities or related electronic equipment for the electronic storage of the communication.
1 1908 (d) "Internet service provider" means the same as that term is defined in Section 76-5c-401.
1 1910 (e) "Prosecutor" means the same as that term is defined in Section 77-22-4.5.
1 1911 (f) "Remote computing service" means the provision to the public of computer storage or processing
services by means of an electronic communications system.
1 1913 (g)
(i) "Sexual offense against a minor" means:
1 1914 (A) sexual exploitation of a minor or attempted sexual exploitation of a minor in violation of

1 1916 (B) aggravated sexual exploitation of a minor or attempted aggravated sexual exploitation of a

1 1918 (C) a sexual offense or attempted sexual offense committed against a minor in violation of Title 76,
Chapter 5, Part 4, Sexual Offenses;
1 1920 (D) dealing in or attempting to deal in material harmful to a minor in violation of Section 76-5c-205
or 76-5c-206;
1 1922 (E) human trafficking of a child in violation of Section 76-5-308.5; or
1 1923 (F) aggravated sexual extortion of a child in violation of Section 76-5b-204.
1 1924 (ii) "Sexual offense against a minor" does not include an offense described in Section 76-5-418,
76-5-419, or 76-5-420.
1 1926 (2) When a law enforcement agency is investigating a sexual offense against a minor, an offense of
stalking under Section 76-5-106.5, or an offense of child kidnapping under Section 76-5-301.1, and
has reasonable suspicion that an electronic communications system or service or remote computing
service has been used in the commission of a criminal offense, a law enforcement agent shall:
1 1931 (a) articulate specific facts showing reasonable grounds to believe that the records or other information
sought, as designated in Subsections (2)(c)(i) through (v), are relevant and material to an ongoing
investigation;
1 1934 (b) present the request to a prosecutor for review and authorization to proceed; and
- 367 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 1935 (c) submit the request to a magistrate for a court order, [consistent] in accordance with 18 U.S.C.
Sec. 2703 and 18 U.S.C. Sec. 2702, to the electronic communications system or service or remote
computing service provider that owns or controls the [Internet] internet protocol address, websites,
email address, or service to a specific telephone number, requiring the production of the following
information, if available, upon providing in the court order the [Internet] internet protocol address,
email address, telephone number, or other identifier, and the dates and times the address, telephone
number, or other identifier is suspected of being used in the commission of the offense:
1 1944 (i) names of subscribers, service customers, and users;
1 1945 (ii) addresses of subscribers, service customers, and users;
1 1946 (iii) records of session times and durations;
1 1947 (iv) length of service, including the start date and types of service utilized; and
1 1948 (v) telephone or other instrument subscriber numbers or other subscriber identifiers, including a
temporarily assigned network address.
1 1950 (3) A court order issued under this section shall state that the electronic communications system or
service or remote computing service provider shall produce a record under Subsections (2)(c)(i)
through (v) that is reasonably relevant to the investigation of the suspected criminal activity or
offense as described in the court order.
1 1954 (4)
(a) An electronic communications system or service or remote computing service provider that provides
information in response to a court order issued under this section may charge a fee, not to exceed the
actual cost, for providing the information.
1 1957 (b) The law enforcement agency conducting the investigation shall pay the fee.
1 1958 (5) The electronic communications system or service or remote computing service provider served with
or responding to the court order may not disclose the court order to the account holder identified
[pursuant to] in accordance with the court order for a period of 90 days.
1 1962 (6) If the electronic communications system or service or remote computing service provider served
with the court order does not own or control the [Internet] internet protocol address, websites, or
email address, or provide service for the telephone number that is the subject of the court order, the
provider shall notify the investigating law enforcement agency that the provider does not have the
information.
1 1967

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01

the provider or service's officers, employees, agents, or other specified persons, for providing

section or statutory authorization.

1 2066 (1)
(a) There is created the Board of Pardons and Parole.
1 2067 (b) The board shall consist of five full-time members and not more than five pro tempore members to
be appointed by the governor with the advice and consent of the Senate in accordance with Title
63G, Chapter 24, Part 2, Vacancies, and as provided in this section.
1 2071 (c) The members of the board shall be resident citizens of the state.

the Legislature in Title 67, Chapter 22, State Officer Compensation.
1 2074 (2)
(a)
(i)
(A) The full-time board members shall serve terms of five years.
1 2075 (B) The terms of the full-time members shall be staggered so one board member is appointed
for a term of five years on March 1 of each year.
1 2077 (ii)
(A) The pro tempore members shall serve terms of five years, beginning on March 1 of the year of
appointment, with no more than one pro tempore member term beginning or expiring in the same
calendar year.
1 2080 (B) If a pro tempore member vacancy occurs, the board may submit the names of not fewer than three
or more than five [persons] individuals to the governor for appointment to fill the vacancy.
1 2083 (b) All vacancies occurring on the board for any cause shall be filled by the governor with the advice
and consent of the Senate in accordance with this section for the unexpired term of the vacating
member.
1 2086 (c) The governor may at any time remove any member of the board for inefficiency, neglect of duty,
malfeasance or malfeasance in office, or for cause upon a hearing.
1 2088 (d)
(i) A member of the board may not hold any other office in the government of the United States, this
state or any other state, or of any county government or municipal corporation within a state.
1 2091 (ii) A member may not engage in any occupation or business inconsistent with the member's duties.
1 2093 (e)

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01

1 2098 (A) parole, pardon, commutation, or termination of a sentence is granted in an offender's case;

policies of general applicability as provided by statute.

remaining board members to exercise any duty or authority of the board as long as a majority of the

1 2355 Section 243. Section 77-27-5.4 is amended to read:
1 2356 77-27-5.4. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Earned time program.
1 2269 (1) The board shall establish an earned time program that reduces the period of incarceration for
offenders who successfully complete specified programs, the purpose of which is to reduce the risk
of recidivism.
1 2272 (2) The earned time program shall:
1 2273 (a) provide not less than four months of earned time credit each for the completion of up to two
programs that:
1 2275 (i) are approved by the board in collaboration with the department; and
1 2276 (ii) are recommended programs that are part of the offender's case action plan; and
1 2277 (b) allow the board to grant in the board's discretion earned time credit in addition to the earned time
credit provided under Subsection (2)(a).
1 2279 (3) The earned time program may not provide earned time credit for an offender:
1 2280 (a) whose previously ordered release date does not provide enough time, including time for transition
services, for the board to grant the earned time credit;
1 2282 (b) who has been sentenced by the court to a term of life without the possibility of parole;
1 2283 (c) who has been ordered by the board to serve until the expiration of the offender's sentence, including
a life sentence;
1 2285 (d) who does not have a current release date;
1 2286 (e) who has not met a contingency requirement for release that has been ordered by the board; or
1 2288 (f) who has been given a termination date by the board.
- 378 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 2289 (4) The board may order the forfeiture of earned time credits under this section if the board determines
a rescission hearing is necessary.
1 2291 (5) The department shall notify the board not more than 30 days after an offender completes a program
as defined in Subsection (2)(a).
1 2293 (6) The board shall collect data for the fiscal year regarding the operation of the earned time credit
program, including:
1 2295 (a) the number of offenders who have earned time credit under this section in the prior year;
1 2297 (b) the amount of time credit earned in the prior year;
1 2298 (c) the number of offenders who forfeited earned time credit; and
1 2299 (d) additional related information as requested by the [Commission on Criminal and Juvenile
Justice] Department of Criminal Justice.
1 2301 (7) The board shall collaborate with the department in the establishment of the earned time credit
program.
1 2303 (8) To the extent possible, programming and hearings shall be provided early enough in an offender's
incarceration to allow the offender to earn time credit.

1 2326 (ii) The original agreement shall remain with the board's file.

(a) If an offender convicted of violating or attempting to violate Section 76-5-301.1, 76-5-302,
76-5-402, 76-5-402.1, 76-5-402.2, 76-5-402.3, 76-5-403, 76-5-403.1, 76-5-404, 76-5-404.1,
76-5-404.3, or 76-5-405, is released on parole, the board shall order outpatient mental health
counseling and treatment as a condition of parole.
1 2331 (b) The board shall develop standards and conditions of parole under this Subsection (2) in accordance
with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act.
1 2333 (c) This Subsection (2) does not apply to intensive early release parole.
1 2334 (3)
(a)
(i) In addition to the conditions set out in Subsection (1), the board may place offenders in an
intensive early release parole program.
1 2336 (ii) The board shall determine the conditions of parole which are reasonably necessary to protect the
community as well as to protect the interests of the offender and to assist the offender to lead a
law-abiding life.
1 2339 (b) The offender is eligible for this program only if the offender:
1 2340 (i) has not been convicted of a sexual offense; or
1 2341 (ii) has not been sentenced [pursuant to] in accordance with Section 76-3-406.
1 2342 (c) The department shall:
1 2343 (i) make rules in accordance with Title 63G, Chapter 3, Utah Administrative Rulemaking Act, for
operation of the program;
1 2345 (ii) adopt and implement internal management policies for operation of the program;
1 2346 (iii) determine whether[ or not] to refer an offender into this program within 120 days from the date the
offender is committed to prison by the sentencing court; and
1 2348
- 380 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(iv) make the final recommendation to the board regarding the placement of an offender into the
program.
1 2350 (d) The department may not consider credit for time served in a county jail awaiting trial or sentencing
when calculating the 120-day period.
1 2352 (e) The prosecuting attorney or sentencing court may refer an offender for consideration by the
department for participation in the program.
1 2354 (f) The board shall determine whether[ or not] to place an offender into this program within 30 days of
receiving the department's recommendation.
1 2356 (4) This program shall be implemented by the department within the existing budget.
1 2357 (5) In addition to the conditions of parole described in this section, and if a condition of the offender's
parole is routine or random drug testing, the board shall order the offender to sign a waiver
consistent with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, 42 U.S.C. Sec. 1320d et
seq., allowing the treatment provider conducting the drug testing to notify the offender's supervising
parole officer regarding the results of the offender's drug testing.
1 2363 (6) During the time the offender is on parole, the department shall collect from the offender the monthly
supervision fee authorized by Section 64-14-204.
1 2365 (7) When a parolee commits a violation of the parole agreement, the department may:
1 2366 (a) respond in accordance with the graduated and evidence-based responses established in accordance
with Section 64-14-204; or
1 2368 (b) when the graduated and evidence-based responses established in accordance with Section 64-14-204
indicate, refer the parolee to the Board of Pardons and Parole for revocation of parole.

1 2782 (iii) Section 76-5d-208 regarding aggravated exploitation of prostitution[, the court may, during any
court hearing where the defendant is present, issue a pretrial criminal no contact order:] .
1 2785 (b) The pretrial criminal no contact order described in Subsection (14)(a) may:
1 2786 (i) [prohibiting ] prohibit the defendant from harassing, telephoning, contacting, or otherwise
communicating with the victim directly or through a third party;
1 2788 (ii) [ordering] order the defendant to stay away from the residence, school, place of employment of
the victim, and the premises of any of these, or any specified place frequented by the victim or any
designated family member of the victim directly or through a third party; and
1 2792 (iii) [ordering] order any other relief that the court considers necessary to protect and provide for the
safety of the victim and any designated family or household member of the victim.
1 2795 [(b)] (c) Violation of a pretrial criminal no contact order issued [pursuant to] in accordance with this
section is a third degree felony.
1 2797 [(c)] (d)
(i) The court shall provide to the victim a certified copy of any pretrial criminal no contact order that
has been issued if the victim can be located with reasonable effort.
1 2800 (ii) The court shall also transmit the pretrial criminal no contact order to the statewide domestic
violence network in accordance with Section 78B-7-113.

the victim of that possibility as soon as practicable.

1 2894 Section 251. Section 77-38-11 is amended to read:
1 2895 77-38-11. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Enforcement -- Appellate review -- No
right to money damages.
1 2809 (1) If a person acting under color of state law allegedly violates the rights of a victim described in this
chapter, Chapter 37, Victims' Rights, or Utah Constitution, Article I, Section 28, the victim, or
a representative of a victim, may file a complaint with a victim rights committee as described in
Section [63M-7-1003] 75E-6-303.
1 2813 (2) If [a person] an individual acting under color of state law willfully or wantonly fails to perform
duties so that the rights in this chapter are not provided, an action for injunctive relief, including
prospective injunctive relief, may be brought against the individual and the governmental entity that
employs the individual.
1 2817 (3)
(a) The victim of a crime or representative of a victim of a crime may:
1 2818 (i) bring an action for declaratory relief or for a writ of mandamus defining or enforcing the rights
of victims and the obligations of government entities under this chapter;
1 2821 (ii) petition to file an amicus brief in any court in any case affecting crime victims; and
1 2823 (iii) after giving notice to the prosecution and the defense, seek an appropriate remedy for a
violation of a victim's right from the court assigned to the case involving the issue.
1 2826 (b) Adverse rulings on these actions or on a motion or request brought by a victim of a crime or
a representative of a victim of a crime may be appealed under the rules governing appellate
actions, provided that an appeal may not constitute grounds for delaying any criminal or juvenile
proceeding.
1 2830 (c) An appellate court shall review all properly presented issues, including issues that are capable of
repetition but would otherwise evade review.
1 2832 (4)
(a) Upon a showing that the victim has not unduly delayed in seeking to protect the victim's right, and
after hearing from the prosecution and the defense, the court shall determine whether a right of the
victim has been violated.
1 2835 (b) If the court determines that a victim's right has been violated, the court shall:
- 395 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 2836 (i) determine the appropriate remedy for the violation of the victim's right by hearing from the victim
and the parties and considering all factors relevant to the issue; and
1 2838 (ii) award an appropriate remedy to the victim.
1 2839 (5)
(a) The court shall:
1 2840 (i) reconsider any judicial decision or judgment affected by a violation of the victim's right; and
1 2842 (ii) upon affording the victim the right and further hearing from the prosecution and the defense,
determine whether the decision or judgment would have been different.
1 2845 (b) If the court's decision or judgment would have been different, the court shall enter the new different
decision or judgment as the appropriate remedy.
1 2847 (c) If necessary to protect the victim's right, the court shall enter the new decision or judgment nunc pro
tunc to the time the first decision or judgment was reached.
1 2849 (d) In no event shall the appropriate remedy be a new trial, damages, attorney fees, or costs.
1 2851 (6)
(a) The appropriate remedy under Subsection (4) or (5) shall include only actions necessary to provide
the victim the right to which the victim was entitled.
1 2853 (b) The appropriate remedy under Subsection (4) or (5) may include reopening previously held
proceedings.
1 2855 (7)
(a) Subject to Subsection (7)(c), the court may reopen a sentence or a previously entered guilty or no
contest plea only if reopening the sentence or plea:
1 2857 (i) would not preclude continued prosecution or sentencing the defendant; and
1 2858 (ii) would not otherwise permit the defendant to escape justice.
1 2859 (b) The court shall tailor a remedy to provide the victim with an appropriate remedy without violating
any constitutional right of the defendant.
1 2861 (c) If the court sets aside a previously entered plea of guilty or no contest, and the continued prosecution
of the charge is held to be prevented by the defendant's having been previously put in jeopardy, the
order setting aside the plea is void and the plea is reinstated as of the date of the plea's original entry.
1 2865 (d) The court may not award as a remedy the dismissal of any criminal charge.
1 2866 (e) The court may not award any remedy if the proceeding that the victim is challenging occurred more
than 90 days before the day on which the victim filed an action alleging the violation of the right.
- 396 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 2869 (8) The failure to provide the rights in this chapter or Chapter 37, Victims' Rights, shall not constitute
cause for a judgment against the state or any government entity, or any individual employed by the
state or any government entity, for monetary damages, attorney fees, or the costs of exercising any
rights under this chapter.
1 2873 (9) A defendant convicted of an offense may not bring an action or complaint concerning a violation of
this chapter or Chapter 37, Victims' Rights.
1 2963 Section 252. Section 77-38-302 is amended to read:
1 2964 77-38-302. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Definitions.
As used in this part:
1 2878 (1) "Convicted person" means [a person] an individual who has been convicted of a crime.
1 2879 (2) "Conviction" means an adjudication by a federal or state court resulting from a trial or plea,
including a plea of no contest, nolo contendere, a finding of not guilty due to insanity, or not guilty
but having a mental condition regardless of whether the sentence was imposed or suspended.
1 2883 (3) "Fund" means the Crime Victim [Reparations] Compensation Fund created in Section
[63M-7-526] 75E-5-302.
1 2885 (4) "Memorabilia" means any tangible property of a convicted person or a representative or assignee
of a convicted person, the value of which is enhanced by the notoriety gained from the criminal
activity for which the person was convicted.
1 2888 (5) "Notoriety of crimes contract" means a contract or other agreement with a convicted person, or a
representative or assignee of a convicted person, with respect to:
1 2890 (a) the reenactment of a crime in any manner including a movie, book, magazine article,
[Internet] internet website, recording, phonograph record, radio or television presentation, or live
entertainment of any kind;
1 2893 (b) the expression of the convicted person's thoughts, feelings, opinions, or emotions regarding a crime
involving or causing personal injury, death, or property loss as a direct result of the crime; or
1 2896 (c) the payment or exchange of any money or other consideration or the proceeds or profits that directly
or indirectly result from the notoriety of the crime.
1 2898 (6) "Office" means the [Utah ]Office for Victims of Crime.
1 2899 (7) "Profit" means any income or benefit:
1 2900 (a) over and above the fair market value of tangible property that is received upon the sale or transfer of
memorabilia; or
- 397 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
1 2902 (b) any money, negotiable instruments, securities, or other consideration received or contracted for gain
which is traceable to a notoriety of crimes contract.
1 2992 Section 253. Section 77-38-303 is amended to read:
1 2993 77-38-303. {(Effective 05/06/26)}(Effective 07/01/26)Profit from sale of memorabilia or
notoriety of crimes contract -- Deposit into Crime Victim Compensation Fund -- Penalty.
1 2907 (1) Any convicted person or a representative or assignee of a convicted person who receives a profit
from the sale or transfer of memorabilia shall remit to the fund:
1 2909 (a) a complete, itemized accounting of the transaction, including:
1 2910 (i) a description of each item sold;
1 2911 (ii) the amount received for each item;
1 2912 (iii) the estimated fair market value of each item; and
1 2913 (iv) the name and address of the purchaser of each item; and
1 2914 (b) a check or money order for the amount of the profit, which shall be the difference between the
amount received for the item and the estimated fair market value of the item.
1 2917 (2) Any person who willfully violates Subsection (1) may be assessed a civil penalty of up to $1,000 per
item sold or transferred or three times the amount of the unremitted profit, whichever is greater.
1 2920 (3)
(a) Any person or entity who enters into a notoriety of crime contract with a convicted person or with a
representative or assignee of a convicted person shall pay to the fund any profit which by the terms
of the contract would otherwise be owing to the convicted person or representative or assignee of
the convicted person.
1 2924 (b) A convicted person or a representative or assignee of a convicted person who has received any profit
from a notoriety of crime contract shall remit the profit to the fund.[ ]
1 2927 (c) Any future profit which, by the terms of the contract, would otherwise be owing to the convicted
person or a representative or assignee of a convicted person shall be paid to the fund as required
under Subsection (3)(a).
1 2930 (4)
(a) Upon receipt of money under Subsection (3), the office shall distribute the amounts to the victim of
the crime from which the profits are derived if any restitution remains outstanding.[ ]
1 2933 (b) If no restitution is outstanding, the money shall be deposited into the fund.
1 2934 (5)
- 398 -

SB0323 compared with SB0323S01
(a) Any person or entity who willfully violates Subsection (3) may be assessed a civil penalty of up
to $1,000,000.00, or up to three times the total value of the original notoriety of crime contract,
whichever is greater.
1 2937 (b) Any civil penalty ordered under this Subsection shall be paid to the fund.
1 2938 (6) The prosecuting agency or the attorney general may bring an action to enforce the provisions of this
chapter in the court of conviction.
1 2940 (7) A court shall enter an order to remit funds as provided in this chapter if [it] the court finds by a
preponderance of the evidence any violation of Subsection (1) or (3).

1 3191 (23) "Plea in abeyance" means an order by a court, upon motion of the prosecuting attorney and the
